best_plan.title: TIP Capital Project Management System - Round 2
best_plan.executive_summary: Django REST Framework API on Cloud Run with React 18 frontend managing TIP capital project submission, review, and federal reporting for a metropolitan planning organization with SAML federation to 12 member agency IdPs.
best_plan.architecture_overview: Django monolith on Cloud Run with Cloud SQL PostgreSQL 16. SAML 2.0 with python3-saml for agency authentication. React 18 SPA for planner and reviewer workflows.

target_output.thinking_summary: Round 3 focuses on resolving ISS-002 (23 CFR Part 450 export field mappings) and ISS-003 (unauthenticated public comment form CSRF). For ISS-002, I need to specify the exact federal_export_row schema mapping data model fields to 23 CFR Part 450 Appendix C column names and add the export regression test to CI. For ISS-003, I need to explicitly describe how the React frontend obtains the Django csrftoken cookie and includes it as X-CSRFToken header on the unauthenticated POST request. Both are targeted fixes with minimal architectural change.

fix_report.action_taken: Added federal_export_row schema with explicit field-to-column mappings in data_model section and documented export generation logic in api_design. Added export format regression test to Cloud Build pipeline in deployment_and_operations.
fix_report.expected_outcome: FHWA submission validator passes without manual post-processing; CI catches format regressions before staging.

fix_report.action_taken: Added explicit CSRF token flow for the unauthenticated public comment form in security_and_compliance: React frontend calls GET /api/v1/csrf/ to obtain the csrftoken cookie, then includes X-CSRFToken header on POST /api/v1/public/comments/.
fix_report.expected_outcome: Django CSRF middleware validates all public comment POST requests; CSRF protection complete for unauthenticated submission endpoint.

target_output.title: TIP Capital Project Management System - MPO Architecture
target_output.executive_summary: A Django REST Framework API and React 18 SPA deployed on GCP Cloud Run managing the full Transportation Improvement Program lifecycle for a metropolitan planning organization: capital project submission by member agency planners via SAML-federated authentication, MPO staff review and prioritization workflows, public comment period management, and automated federal 23 CFR Part 450 Appendix C export generation. Backed by PostgreSQL 16 on Cloud SQL with field-mapped federal exports and complete CSRF protection including unauthenticated public comment endpoints.
target_output.architecture_overview: Django monolith with domain-separated apps (projects, review, comments, exports, accounts) deployed to Cloud Run with min-instances=1 to eliminate cold-start latency for agency planners. React 18 SPA with React Router v6 provides separate route groups for authenticated agency workflows (/app) and the public comment submission form (/public). SAML 2.0 implemented via python3-saml with per-IdP metadata stored in a saml_identity_providers table; email-domain matching routes SSO requests to the correct member agency IdP. Cloud SQL PostgreSQL 16 primary with read replica serves reporting queries. Cloud Armor WAF fronts all Cloud Run ingress. Federal export module generates 23 CFR Part 450 Appendix C compliant CSV with field mappings validated by FHWA submission format.
target_output.technology_stack: Frontend: React 18, TypeScript, React Router v6, React Query, Zustand, Tailwind CSS. Backend: Python 3.12, Django 5, Django REST Framework, python3-saml, djangorestframework-simplejwt, Celery for async export jobs. Database: PostgreSQL 16 on Cloud SQL. Infrastructure: GCP Cloud Run, Cloud SQL, Cloud Armor WAF, Artifact Registry, Cloud Build. Observability: Google Cloud Monitoring, Cloud Logging with structured JSON.
target_output.functional_feature_map: Capital Project Submission: multi-step form capturing project_title, project_type, location fields (address, county, route), total_cost, funding_sources with federal_share_pct per program, and federal_categories (work_type_code, asset_class, improvement_type). MPO Review Queue: sortable table of submitted projects with review scoring form and status FSM transitions (submitted -> under_review -> approved/rejected -> tip_included). TIP Amendment Workflow: amendment number assignment, project list modification, approval tracking. Public Comment Period: open/close comment windows, public unauthenticated submission form with CSRF protection, submitter PII anonymization after period close. Federal Export: one-click 23 CFR Part 450 Appendix C CSV generation per TIP year. Admin Panel: SAML IdP metadata configuration for each member agency, user role assignment.
target_output.system_components: Cloud Armor WAF (ingress DDoS and rate limiting), Cloud Run API service (Django 5 application, min 1 instance), Cloud Run Frontend service (React SPA or static via CDN), Cloud SQL PostgreSQL 16 (primary + read replica), Cloud Build pipeline, Artifact Registry (Docker images), python3-saml SP (SAML 2.0 service provider, per-IdP metadata routing), Celery worker (async federal export generation), Cloud Storage bucket (generated export file staging), Google Cloud Monitoring (metrics and alerting).
target_output.workflows: Agency Project Submission: Agency planner hits /app, auth guard detects no session, backend reads email-domain from login form input, looks up saml_identity_providers by email_domain column, returns IdP SSO URL, browser redirects to agency IdP, SAML assertion returned to /saml/acs/, python3-saml validates assertion, Django creates session with agency_id and agency_planner role, planner completes multi-step project form, form auto-saves draft to projects table on each step, final submit transitions status from draft to submitted and notifies MPO reviewer via email. Federal Export Generation: MPO admin clicks 'Generate STIP Export' for TIP year 2026, API triggers Celery task export_tip_year.delay(2026), Celery queries capital_projects joined with funding_sources and federal_categories where tip_year=2026 AND status=tip_included, maps each row to 23 CFR Part 450 Appendix C columns (see data_model), writes CSV to Cloud Storage, returns signed URL for download; Cloud Build regression test validates column headers and required-field completeness against expected schema on every deploy.
target_output.data_model: capital_projects (id UUID PK, tip_year INT, project_title VARCHAR(200), submitting_agency_id FK agencies, project_type ENUM road/bridge/transit/bike_ped, location_address, location_county, location_route, total_cost NUMERIC(14,2), status ENUM draft/submitted/under_review/approved/rejected/tip_included, submitted_at, reviewed_by FK users, review_notes). funding_sources (id, project_id FK, funding_program ENUM nhpp/stp/cmaq/tap/local, federal_share_pct NUMERIC(5,2) CHECK 0-100, amount NUMERIC(14,2)). federal_categories (id, project_id FK, work_type_code VARCHAR(10), asset_class VARCHAR(50), improvement_type VARCHAR(50)). Federal export row mapping to 23 CFR Part 450 Appendix C: Column 'ProjectTitle' <- project_title; 'TIPYear' <- tip_year; 'ProjectType' <- project_type; 'FundingProgram' <- funding_sources.funding_program; 'TotalProjectCost' <- total_cost; 'FederalShare' <- funding_sources.amount * federal_share_pct/100; 'WorkTypeCode' <- federal_categories.work_type_code; 'AssetClass' <- federal_categories.asset_class. public_comments (id, comment_period_id FK, submitter_name, submitter_email, comment_text, submitted_at, anonymized_at NULLABLE). tip_amendments (id, tip_year, amendment_number INT, description, approved_at, projects UUID[]).
target_output.api_design: DRF ViewSets with OpenAPI schema. Agency-authenticated: POST /api/v1/projects/ (submit project), GET /api/v1/projects/ (list own projects), PATCH /api/v1/projects/{id}/ (update draft). MPO-authenticated: GET /api/v1/review/queue/?status=submitted (review queue), POST /api/v1/review/{project_id}/decision/ (approve/reject), POST /api/v1/exports/tip/{year}/ (trigger Celery export job), GET /api/v1/exports/{export_id}/download/ (signed URL). Public unauthenticated: GET /api/v1/csrf/ (sets csrftoken cookie; React frontend calls this on /public page mount), POST /api/v1/public/comments/ (X-CSRFToken header required; Django CsrfViewMiddleware validates token), GET /api/v1/tip/{year}/projects/ (read-only public project listing for transparency). SAML endpoints: GET /saml/login/?email=user@agency.gov (domain lookup + IdP redirect), POST /saml/acs/ (assertion consumer service).
target_output.security_and_compliance: SAML 2.0 with python3-saml: SP metadata at /saml/metadata/. IdP routing: user enters email at login, backend queries saml_identity_providers WHERE email_domain = domain_part; redirects to IdP sso_url. Assertion validates NameID and agency_id attribute; session created with agency_planner or mpo_reviewer role. CSRF: Django CsrfViewMiddleware active globally. Unauthenticated public comment form: React on /public page mount calls GET /api/v1/csrf/ which triggers Django to set csrftoken cookie; React reads document.cookie for csrftoken, includes it as X-CSRFToken request header on POST /api/v1/public/comments/; Django middleware validates header matches cookie. Cloud Armor WAF: rate limit 100 requests/min per IP on public comment endpoint. 23 CFR Part 450 compliance: federal export field mappings validated against FHWA format; Title VI demographic analysis deferred to Phase 3. Audit log: append-only project_audit_log and export_audit_log tables with DB-level insert-only trigger on rows.
target_output.deployment_and_operations: Cloud Build pipeline: (1) pytest --cov=. --cov-fail-under=80, (2) run export_format_regression_test.py asserting expected 23 CFR Part 450 Appendix C column headers in test export CSV, (3) docker build, (4) push to Artifact Registry, (5) gcloud run deploy api --no-traffic --revision-suffix=$BUILD_ID, (6) run smoke tests against new revision, (7) gcloud run services update-traffic api --to-revisions=$BUILD_ID=100. Environments: dev (Cloud Run dev service, Cloud SQL dev instance), staging (full isolation, test SAML IdP via Keycloak container), production (Cloud SQL HA, 12 real member agency SAML IdPs, Cloud Armor enabled). Rollback: gcloud run services update-traffic api --to-revisions=PREV_REVISION=100.
target_output.observability: Google Cloud Monitoring dashboard: Cloud Run request latency (p50/p95/p99), error rate, active instances. Custom metrics: active_tip_submissions_count (projects in submitted/under_review status by tip_year), comment_period_submission_rate (comments/hour during open periods), federal_export_generation_duration_seconds. Cloud Logging structured JSON with correlation IDs for all API requests. Alert policies: error_rate > 0.5% for 5 minutes, export generation duration > 120 seconds, SAML assertion failure spike > 10/min.
target_output.cost_and_scaling: Cloud Run: ~$30/month with min-instances=1 and moderate agency planner traffic. Cloud SQL PostgreSQL 16 HA with read replica: ~$180/month. Cloud Armor: ~$5/month. Cloud Build: within free tier for ~50 builds/month. Total: ~$220/month. Scaling: Cloud Run scales to max 10 instances on concurrent submission peaks (end-of-cycle submission deadlines); Cloud SQL read replica absorbs reporting and review queue queries during MPO staff review sessions.
target_output.phased_implementation: Phase 1 MVP (sprints 1-6): Django project scaffolding, SAML multi-IdP integration with saml_identity_providers routing, capital project CRUD with funding sources, MPO review queue and status FSM, basic federal export with 23 CFR Part 450 Appendix C field mappings, Cloud Build pipeline with export regression test. Phase 2 (sprints 7-10): Public comment period management with CSRF-protected unauthenticated form, submitter PII anonymization Celery job, TIP amendment workflow, Django admin panel for SAML IdP configuration. Phase 3 (sprints 11-14): GIS project location map (pending platform procurement), Title VI equity analysis module, state DOT STIP integration API.
target_output.development_guidelines: Django app-per-domain structure enforced; no cross-app model imports except via service layer functions. All API mutation endpoints require explicit RBAC permission class; no view-level permission bypasses. Export field mapping dictionary maintained in exports/constants.py with inline comment referencing 23 CFR Part 450 Appendix C column names; any change requires PR with FHWA format validation evidence. SAML assertion handling covered by integration tests using a Keycloak test IdP in CI. Unauthenticated endpoints annotated with @csrf_required explicitly in code for review visibility.
target_output.risks_and_tradeoffs: Risk: Member agency IdP metadata changes (certificate rotation, SSO URL update) cause SAML authentication failures. Mitigation: per-IdP metadata in saml_identity_providers table allows admin to update via admin panel without code deploy. Risk: 23 CFR Part 450 Appendix C format changes between FHWA guidance updates. Mitigation: export format regression test in CI catches column name changes; exports/constants.py provides single update point. Tradeoff: Django monolith over microservices trades service isolation for development speed appropriate to a small MPO engineering team. Tradeoff: Celery for export generation over synchronous response means large exports (thousands of projects) do not block the API but require polling for download URL.
target_output.open_questions_resolved: Confirmed email-domain SAML IdP routing satisfies 12-agency federation requirement. Confirmed GET /api/v1/csrf/ endpoint pattern for unauthenticated CSRF cookie issuance. Confirmed Celery async pattern for federal export generation to avoid Cloud Run request timeout on large datasets. GIS integration deferred to Phase 3 pending budget.

metadata.notes: Round 3 revision resolving federal export field mappings (ISS-002) and unauthenticated CSRF token flow (ISS-003) for a metropolitan planning organization TIP management system.

profile.domain: Municipal Government - City Building Department Permitting Portal

project_goal.value: Build a permitting portal for a city building department decomposed into microservices, handling residential and commercial permit applications, plan review workflows, inspection scheduling, and certificate of occupancy issuance, replacing a legacy monolithic system.
project_goal.rationale: Legacy monolith cannot scale during peak construction season; microservices enable independent deployment of plan review and inspection scheduling without full system downtime.

target_users.value: Contractors and homeowners applying for permits, building department plan reviewers, field inspectors scheduling and recording inspections, and building department administrators.
target_users.rationale: Four primary user roles identified in department workflow analysis.

project_class.rationale: Interactive multi-role permitting application with independent microservice backends.

capabilities.value: frontend, backend, data, auth, analytics, integrations, admin_panel, devops
capabilities.rationale: Multi-service backend, React frontend, analytics for permit volume reporting, contractor license integration, admin panel for fee schedule management.

complexity_level.rationale: Microservices decomposition across permit, plan review, inspection, and certificate domains with cross-service workflows.

risk_level.rationale: Processes personal data (applicant contact info, property addresses) and fee payments; no health or financial account data.

data_sensitivity.rationale: Stores applicant name, address, phone, email, and contractor license numbers.

external_exposure.rationale: Public applicants (contractors, homeowners) apply from outside city network.

access_model.value: Azure AD B2C for public applicant registration and authentication. Azure AD for building department staff. RBAC roles: applicant, plan_reviewer, field_inspector, department_admin.
access_model.rationale: B2C handles external public registration; Azure AD manages internal staff identity already provisioned by city IT.

feature_scope.value: Permit application submission with plan set upload, parallel plan review queue routing (structural, electrical, fire), inspection scheduling with inspector calendar, field inspection result recording, certificate of occupancy generation, fee schedule calculation and payment, contractor license verification via state database API.
feature_scope.rationale: Covers full permit lifecycle replacing all legacy system functions.

mvp_scope.value: Residential permit applications, single-discipline plan review, inspection scheduling, basic fee calculation. Commercial permits and parallel review in phase 2.
mvp_scope.rationale: Residential permits are 80% of volume and lower complexity; validate workflow before tackling parallel commercial review.

security_baseline.value: Azure AD B2C with MFA for public applicants. TLS 1.3 in transit. AKS network policies restricting inter-service communication. RBAC enforced at API Gateway and service level.
security_baseline.rationale: Public-facing application handling personal data requires MFA and network-level isolation between services.

frontend_stack.value: Next.js 14 with TypeScript and React Query.
frontend_stack.rationale: Next.js SSR improves SEO for public permit status lookup pages; React Query manages multi-service API calls.

backend_stack.value: Node.js 22 with Fastify for each microservice.
backend_stack.rationale: Fastify's low overhead suits containerized microservices; consistent language across all services reduces context switching.

data_platform.value: PostgreSQL 16 per-service database on Azure Database for PostgreSQL Flexible Server.
data_platform.rationale: Database-per-service pattern enforces service boundary isolation; managed flexible server provides HA without DBA overhead.

hosting_target.value: Azure Kubernetes Service (AKS) with Azure API Management as gateway.
hosting_target.rationale: AKS provides container orchestration for multiple services; API Management handles routing, rate limiting, and JWT validation.

privacy_retention_policy.value: Permit application records (including applicant PII) retained 10 years per local records retention ordinance. Applicant accounts deletable on request; application records anonymized but retained for building history. Inspection photos retained 5 years.
privacy_retention_policy.rationale: City records retention ordinance; building history needed for future permit and title searches.

future_scope.value: Online fee payment integration via city payment processor. Mobile inspection app for field inspectors. Public permit status map with GIS visualization.
future_scope.rationale: Phase 3 features pending city payment processor contract and GIS platform integration.

constraints.value: Must integrate with state contractor license verification API (read-only, REST). No changes to existing city payment processor integration in phase 1. AKS cluster shared with other city services; resource quotas applied per namespace.
constraints.rationale: State API is external dependency outside city control; payment processor change requires procurement process.

observability_baseline.value: Datadog APM with distributed tracing across services. Structured JSON logging with correlation IDs. Alert on permit service error rate > 1% and inspection scheduling service p99 latency > 3 seconds.
observability_baseline.rationale: Distributed tracing is essential for debugging cross-service permit workflows.

execution_preference.value: Service-by-service delivery: permit service first, then plan review, then inspection, then certificate of occupancy. Each service tested and deployed independently.
execution_preference.rationale: Independent service delivery reduces integration risk and allows earlier user testing of core permit submission workflow.

compliance_context.value: International Building Code compliance tracking for permit conditions. ADA accessibility for public-facing portal. Local records retention ordinance for permit and inspection records. State contractor license verification requirement.
compliance_context.rationale: Building permits must reference applicable code editions; ADA required for public government portal; state law requires license verification before permit issuance.

project.goal: Microservices permitting portal replacing legacy building department monolith
project.target_users: Contractors, homeowners, plan reviewers, field inspectors, department admins

frontend.state: React Query for server state; Zustand for application wizard state
frontend.styling: Tailwind CSS with city design system tokens

backend.api: REST with OpenAPI 3.0 per service; Azure API Management gateway

security.auth: Azure AD B2C (public applicants), Azure AD (staff)
security.transport: TLS 1.3; AKS network policies for east-west traffic
security.rbac: JWT claims-based RBAC at APIM and service level

data.db: PostgreSQL 16 per service via Azure Database for PostgreSQL Flexible Server
data.pattern: database-per-service; no cross-service direct DB access

devops.hosting: AKS with resource quotas per namespace

constraints.state_api: State contractor license API read-only integration
constraints.payment: Existing city payment processor deferred to Phase 3

open_questions.fee_calculation: Fee schedule rules engine or static lookup table TBD in design sprint

reasoner_reviews.product: The service-by-service delivery plan is the right risk mitigation for a microservices migration. Residential permit workflow first is correct given the 80% volume concentration. The parallel plan review routing for commercial permits (structural, electrical, fire reviewers working concurrently) is the architecturally interesting challenge; ensure the plan review service design accounts for this from the start even if phase 1 implements single-discipline only.
reasoner_reviews.architect_reasoner: Database-per-service with PostgreSQL Flexible Server is the correct isolation pattern. The cross-service workflow challenge is the permit status aggregation: when a permit requires sign-off from both plan review and inspection services, the frontend must compose status from multiple service responses or an orchestrating permit service must track cross-service state. This architectural decision needs to be explicit in the plan.
reasoner_reviews.security: Azure AD B2C with MFA for public applicants is well-suited. AKS network policies restricting inter-service calls to declared routes are critical given the shared cluster. Ensure Datadog APM traces include the Azure AD B2C token subject as a trace attribute for audit correlation without logging full PII.
reasoner_reviews.constraints: State contractor license API integration should be wrapped in an internal adapter service or module so that changes to the state API do not require changes across multiple permit service endpoints. AKS resource quotas per namespace must be sized for peak permit submission periods (spring construction season).

backend.service_design: Four microservices in permits namespace: permit-service (application CRUD, fee calculation, contractor license check), plan-review-service (reviewer queue, discipline routing, comment recording), inspection-service (scheduling, inspector availability, result recording), certificate-service (occupancy certificate generation, PDF stamping). Shared libraries: @city/permit-events (Kafka event schemas), @city/auth-middleware (JWT validation).
backend.api_patterns: Azure API Management gateway routes by path prefix: /permits/* -> permit-service, /reviews/* -> plan-review-service, /inspections/* -> inspection-service, /certificates/* -> certificate-service. APIM enforces JWT validation and rate limiting (200 req/min per applicant). Each service exposes OpenAPI 3.0 schema.
backend.business_modules: Permit status orchestration: permit-service is the source of truth for permit.status ENUM (draft/submitted/plan_review/inspection/approved/co_issued/closed). Status transitions triggered by Kafka events from plan-review-service (review_complete) and inspection-service (final_inspection_passed). Contractor license check: permit-service calls state API adapter module, caches result in Redis for 24h to avoid rate limits.
backend.background_jobs: Nightly Azure DevOps scheduled job checks permits with status=inspection and no inspection scheduled in 30 days, sends reminder to applicant. Weekly fee reconciliation job matches paid fees against permit_applications.fee_paid flag.
backend.failure_handling: Kafka event consumer with dead-letter topic for failed status transition events; alert on dead-letter queue depth > 0 for more than 5 minutes. State API adapter circuit breaker: if state API returns 5xx for 3 consecutive requests, fallback to cached license result with 'unverified' flag and notify reviewer.

frontend.app_structure: Next.js 14 with App Router. Route groups: (public) for permit application and status lookup (SSR for SEO), (auth) for applicant dashboard, (staff) for reviewer and inspector workflows, (admin) for fee schedule and user management.
frontend.pages_and_flows: Permit Application Wizard: 4-step form (project type and address, work description with IBC code selection, plan set upload, contractor license entry with live state API lookup). Applicant Dashboard: active permit cards with status timeline, upcoming inspection dates, document download links. Plan Review Queue: discipline-filtered table (structural/electrical/fire), review comment editor with standard comment library. Inspector Schedule: weekly calendar with assigned inspections, mobile-optimized result entry form. Admin: fee schedule editor, department user management, permit report exports.
frontend.state_management: React Query: usePermitList, usePermitDetail, usePlanReviewQueue, useInspectorSchedule. Zustand: application wizard step data and draft auto-save. SSR for public permit status page to support search engine indexing of permit numbers.

security.auth_design: Azure AD B2C tenant for public applicants with email+MFA. Azure AD for building department staff with city SSO. APIM validates JWT from either issuer; claims include roles[] and sub. Staff JWTs include department_role claim (plan_reviewer/field_inspector/department_admin).
security.authorization_model: APIM enforces route-level role requirements (e.g., /reviews/* requires plan_reviewer or department_admin role in JWT claims). Each service performs secondary authorization check using @city/auth-middleware. Inspectors can only update inspection records assigned to their inspector_id.
security.secrets_management: Azure Key Vault stores PostgreSQL connection strings, state API key, B2C client secrets. AKS pods access Key Vault via Workload Identity (no long-lived credentials in container images or env vars). Secrets rotated on 90-day schedule via Key Vault rotation policy.
security.audit_and_logging_controls: Datadog APM spans tagged with permit_id and acting user sub. Permit status transitions written to permit_audit_log table. All inspection result submissions logged with inspector_id, GPS coordinates, timestamp, and outcome.

data.entities: permit-service DB: permit_applications (id UUID, permit_number VARCHAR generated, applicant_id, property_address, permit_type ENUM residential_new/residential_reno/commercial_new/commercial_reno/demolition, work_description, total_valuation, fee_amount, fee_paid BOOL, status ENUM draft/submitted/plan_review/inspection/approved/co_issued/closed, submitted_at). contractor_licenses (id, permit_id, license_number, licensee_name, license_type, expiry_date, verified_at, verification_status ENUM verified/unverified/invalid). plan-review-service DB: review_assignments (id, permit_id, discipline ENUM structural/electrical/fire/zoning, assigned_reviewer_id, status ENUM pending/in_review/approved/corrections_required, assigned_at). review_comments (id, assignment_id, comment_text, ibc_code_reference, required_correction, resolved_at). inspection-service DB: inspection_requests (id, permit_id, inspection_type ENUM framing/electrical_rough/plumbing_rough/final, requested_date, scheduled_date, assigned_inspector_id, status ENUM requested/scheduled/passed/failed/cancelled). inspection_results (id, inspection_request_id, outcome ENUM pass/fail/partial, notes, photos_s3_keys[], recorded_at, gps_lat, gps_lng). certificate-service DB: certificates_of_occupancy (id, permit_id, issued_at, issued_by_id, certificate_number, pdf_storage_url).
data.storage_design: Azure Blob Storage for plan set uploads (per-permit container) and inspection photos. PostgreSQL per service with connection pooling via PgBouncer sidecar. Redis cache for contractor license API responses (TTL 24h).
data.migration_notes: Legacy monolith data migration via extraction scripts to per-service schemas. permit_applications migrated first; historical review and inspection records migrated in parallel with legacy system running read-only during cutover window.

devops.deployment_topology: Azure DevOps Pipelines build and push Docker images to Azure Container Registry. AKS cluster in East US 2 with permits namespace resource quota (4 vCPU, 8 GB RAM). Each service deployed as AKS Deployment with HPA scaling on CPU 60% target. Azure API Management in Consumption tier fronts all services.
devops.ci_cd_design: Per-service pipeline: lint, unit test (Jest 90% coverage gate), integration test against ephemeral PostgreSQL container, docker build, push to ACR, helm upgrade --atomic to AKS staging, smoke tests, promote to production. Services deploy independently; permit-service is the dependency anchor.
devops.observability_stack: Datadog APM with ddtrace auto-instrumentation for Fastify. Distributed traces span permit submission through plan review status update via Kafka event correlation. Custom dashboards: permit application funnel, plan review cycle time by discipline, inspection pass rate by type and inspector.
devops.rollback_strategy: Helm release history per service; helm rollback permits/permit-service 1 reverts to prior release. Kafka event consumers handle out-of-order events via idempotent status FSM to tolerate rollback scenarios.

target_output.thinking_summary: Microservices permitting portal for a city building department with four bounded contexts: permit application, plan review, inspection, and certificate of occupancy. The core architectural challenge is cross-service permit status orchestration when review and inspection domains each contribute to the overall permit lifecycle. Using Kafka events with permit-service as the status aggregate is the right pattern. Azure AD B2C handles public applicants while Azure AD handles internal staff via a shared APIM JWT validation layer.
target_output.title: City Building Department Permitting Portal - Microservices Architecture
target_output.executive_summary: A microservices-based permitting portal on Azure Kubernetes Service replacing a legacy building department monolith, handling residential and commercial permit applications, parallel plan review routing across structural/electrical/fire disciplines, inspection scheduling and result recording, and certificate of occupancy issuance. Four independently deployable services (permit, plan-review, inspection, certificate) communicate via Kafka events, with Azure API Management as the unified gateway. Next.js 14 frontend serves public applicants and department staff through separate route groups with Azure AD B2C and Azure AD respectively.
target_output.architecture_overview: Four Fastify microservices deployed to an AKS permits namespace: permit-service owns the permit lifecycle FSM and is the cross-service status aggregate; plan-review-service manages discipline queues and reviewer assignments; inspection-service handles inspector calendars, scheduling, and field result recording; certificate-service generates and stores certificates of occupancy. Services communicate asynchronously via Kafka: review_complete and final_inspection_passed events trigger permit-service status transitions. Azure API Management gateway routes by path prefix, enforces JWT validation (B2C for public applicants, Azure AD for staff), and applies rate limits. Per-service PostgreSQL databases on Azure Database for PostgreSQL Flexible Server enforce service boundary isolation. Next.js 14 App Router frontend serves SSR permit status pages for public lookup and CSR-rendered authenticated workflows for applicant dashboard and staff queues. Datadog APM provides distributed tracing across all four services via Kafka message correlation IDs.
target_output.technology_stack: Frontend: Next.js 14, TypeScript, React Query, Zustand, Tailwind CSS. Backend: Node.js 22, Fastify 4 (per microservice), Zod for request validation, kafkajs for event publishing/consuming. Shared packages: @city/permit-events (Kafka schema types), @city/auth-middleware (JWT validation). Database: PostgreSQL 16 via Azure Database for PostgreSQL Flexible Server (per service). Cache: Redis 7 (contractor license API response cache). Message Bus: Azure Event Hubs (Kafka-compatible). Container Orchestration: AKS (Azure Kubernetes Service). Gateway: Azure API Management (Consumption tier). Auth: Azure AD B2C (public applicants), Azure AD (staff). Secret Store: Azure Key Vault with Workload Identity. CI/CD: Azure DevOps Pipelines. Observability: Datadog APM, ddtrace, structured JSON logging.
target_output.functional_feature_map: Permit Application Module: 4-step wizard (project type selection including permit_type ENUM residential_new/residential_reno/commercial_new, work description with IBC code reference lookup, plan set upload to Azure Blob, contractor license number with live state API verification). Plan Review Module: discipline-filtered review queue (structural/electrical/fire/zoning), review comment editor with IBC code reference linking, corrections_required -> applicant resubmission cycle, review_complete Kafka event on all disciplines approved. Inspection Module: applicant requests inspection type (framing/electrical_rough/plumbing_rough/final), inspector calendar availability check, scheduled inspection triggers applicant notification, field inspector records pass/fail with photos and GPS coordinates. Certificate of Occupancy Module: certificate-service generates PDF on final_inspection_passed event, department admin issues and stores stamped certificate. Admin Panel: fee schedule editor (permit_type -> valuation_range -> fee lookup table), APIM rate limit configuration, department user role management, permit volume analytics.
target_output.system_components: Azure API Management (unified gateway, JWT validation, rate limiting 200 req/min/applicant), permit-service (Fastify, permit lifecycle FSM, contractor license check, fee calculation), plan-review-service (Fastify, discipline queue management, review comment storage), inspection-service (Fastify, inspector calendar, inspection result recording), certificate-service (Fastify, PDF generation via Puppeteer, Azure Blob upload), Azure Event Hubs (Kafka-compatible message bus for cross-service events), PostgreSQL Flexible Server x4 (per-service databases), Redis Cache (contractor license API response cache, 24h TTL), Azure Blob Storage (plan set uploads, inspection photos, certificate PDFs), Azure AD B2C (public applicant authentication), Azure AD (staff authentication), Azure Key Vault (secrets with Workload Identity), Datadog APM (distributed tracing and dashboards).
target_output.workflows: Residential Permit Submission: Applicant creates B2C account, completes 4-step application wizard; on step 4, permit-service calls state contractor license API (with Redis cache check), validates license is active and matches permit_type; on submission, permit-service creates permit_application with status=submitted, publishes permit_submitted event. Plan Review triggers: plan-review-service consumes permit_submitted, creates review_assignment for structural discipline (phase 1: single discipline), reviewer opens queue, records comments or approves, triggers review_complete event; permit-service transitions permit to inspection. Inspection Scheduling: applicant selects inspection_type=framing on permit dashboard, inspection-service checks inspector availability calendar, reserves slot and returns scheduled_date; inspector records field result (pass/fail, photos, GPS) on mobile-optimized form; final_inspection_passed event triggers certificate-service to generate CO PDF, permit-service transitions status to co_issued.
target_output.data_model: permit-service DB: permit_applications (id UUID PK, permit_number VARCHAR(20) GENERATED as 'BLDG-' || LPAD(nextval, 6), applicant_id from B2C sub, property_address, permit_type ENUM, work_description, total_valuation NUMERIC(12,2), fee_amount NUMERIC(10,2), fee_paid BOOL DEFAULT false, status ENUM draft/submitted/plan_review/inspection/approved/co_issued/closed, submitted_at TIMESTAMPTZ). contractor_licenses (id UUID PK, permit_id FK, license_number, licensee_name, license_type, expiry_date DATE, verified_at TIMESTAMPTZ, verification_status ENUM verified/unverified/invalid). plan-review-service DB: review_assignments (id UUID PK, permit_id UUID, discipline ENUM structural/electrical/fire/zoning, assigned_reviewer_id, status ENUM pending/in_review/approved/corrections_required, assigned_at). review_comments (id UUID PK, assignment_id FK, comment_text, ibc_section_reference, required_correction TEXT, resolved_at TIMESTAMPTZ). inspection-service DB: inspection_requests (id UUID PK, permit_id UUID, inspection_type ENUM framing/electrical_rough/plumbing_rough/final, requested_date DATE, scheduled_date DATE, assigned_inspector_id, status ENUM requested/scheduled/passed/failed/cancelled). inspection_results (id UUID PK, inspection_request_id FK, outcome ENUM pass/fail/partial, field_notes, photos_blob_paths TEXT[], recorded_at TIMESTAMPTZ, gps_lat NUMERIC(9,6), gps_lng NUMERIC(9,6)). certificate-service DB: certificates_of_occupancy (id UUID PK, permit_id UUID, issued_at TIMESTAMPTZ, issued_by_id, certificate_number VARCHAR(20), pdf_blob_url TEXT).
target_output.api_design: Azure APIM routes by path prefix. permit-service: POST /permits/ (create application draft), PUT /permits/{id}/submit (submit with license verification), GET /permits/{id} (status with cross-service aggregation via parallel fetches to plan-review and inspection), GET /permits/?applicant_id= (applicant dashboard list), PATCH /permits/{id}/fee-paid (payment webhook from payment processor). plan-review-service: GET /reviews/?discipline=structural&status=pending (reviewer queue), POST /reviews/{assignment_id}/approve (triggers review_complete event), POST /reviews/{assignment_id}/corrections (records required corrections, notifies applicant). inspection-service: POST /inspections/{permit_id}/request (request inspection type), GET /inspections/availability?date=&type= (inspector calendar slots), POST /inspections/{id}/result (record pass/fail with photo multipart upload), GET /inspections/{permit_id}/history (all inspection records). certificate-service: GET /certificates/{permit_id}/download (signed Azure Blob URL for CO PDF). All services return application/json with correlation_id header for Datadog trace linking.
target_output.security_and_compliance: Azure AD B2C: email + MFA for public applicants; custom policy enforces MFA on first login. Azure AD: city SSO for staff with department_role claim injected via app role assignment. APIM validates both JWT issuers; routes to correct service based on path. AKS network policies: permit-service can call plan-review-service only on /reviews/permits/* health check path; inter-service communication restricted to explicitly declared NetworkPolicy egress rules. Azure Key Vault Workload Identity: pods bind to managed identity; no secrets in environment variables or config maps. Contractor license state API key stored in Key Vault, rotated every 90 days. Datadog APM spans include permit_id and user_sub (not full PII). Inspection GPS coordinates stored but access restricted to department_admin role. IBC compliance tracking: each review_comment records ibc_section_reference; permit cannot advance without all required corrections marked resolved.
target_output.deployment_and_operations: Azure DevOps Pipelines per service: (1) eslint + Jest (90% coverage gate), (2) docker build tagged with commit SHA, (3) push to Azure Container Registry, (4) helm upgrade --install --atomic --set image.tag=$SHA to AKS staging namespace, (5) smoke test suite (POST permit application, verify review assignment created via Kafka consumer), (6) promote to production namespace with helm upgrade. HPA: permit-service scales from 2 to 10 pods on CPU > 60%; plan-review-service from 1 to 5. AKS permits namespace resource quota: 8 vCPU, 16 GB RAM. Rollback: helm rollback permits/permit-service 1. Kafka dead-letter consumer alerts Datadog on permit status event processing failures. Database migrations: per-service migration job as Kubernetes Job pre-upgrade hook.
target_output.observability: Datadog APM: ddtrace auto-instrumentation on all Fastify services. Distributed trace correlation via Kafka message header x-datadog-trace-id. Custom Datadog dashboards: permit application funnel (submitted -> plan_review -> inspection -> co_issued conversion rates), plan review cycle time by discipline (p50/p95 days), inspection pass rate by type and inspector, contractor license verification failure rate. Alerts: permit-service error rate > 1% for 5 minutes, inspection-service p99 request latency > 3 seconds, Kafka dead-letter topic depth > 0 for > 5 minutes, state API circuit breaker open.
target_output.cost_and_scaling: AKS cluster (shared with other city services; permits namespace costs ~$200/month for allocated quota). Azure Database for PostgreSQL Flexible Server x4: ~$80/month per service (Burstable_Standard_B2ms). Azure Event Hubs Standard: ~$25/month. Azure API Management Consumption tier: ~$0.035/10K calls (~$15/month at estimated 400K monthly API calls). Redis Cache: ~$40/month (C1 Standard). Total permits namespace: approximately $600/month. Spring construction season scaling: HPA expands permit-service to 10 pods within 2 minutes of CPU threshold crossing; no pre-warming required.
target_output.phased_implementation: Phase 1 MVP (months 1-6): permit-service with residential permit types and single-discipline structural plan review, inspection scheduling and result recording for framing/final inspections, certificate of occupancy generation, Azure AD B2C and Azure AD auth, APIM gateway, Kafka event bus, Datadog APM. Migrated from legacy system. Phase 2 (months 7-12): Commercial permit types, parallel multi-discipline plan review routing, electrical_rough and plumbing_rough inspection types, fee calculation engine with permit_type/valuation lookup, contractor license caching optimization, department admin panel with fee schedule editor. Phase 3 (months 13-18): Online fee payment integration via city payment processor, mobile-optimized inspection result entry app, GIS permit location map, public permit status map with filterable open permit pins.
target_output.development_guidelines: Each microservice is an independent Node.js package in a monorepo under packages/. Shared types live in @city/permit-events; no direct cross-service imports. Fastify route handlers must not contain business logic; delegate to service layer functions covered by unit tests. Kafka event schemas are versioned in @city/permit-events with backward-compatible additions only. APIM policy changes require PR review from API Management owner. AKS NetworkPolicy changes require security team approval. Permit status FSM state machine defined in permit-service/src/lib/permitFSM.ts and tested for all valid and invalid transitions.
target_output.risks_and_tradeoffs: Risk: State contractor license API outage blocks permit submissions. Mitigation: circuit breaker with 24h Redis cache fallback returns last-known license status with 'unverified' flag allowing reviewer override. Risk: Kafka consumer lag causes stale permit status during peak periods. Mitigation: HPA on inspection-service and plan-review-service Kafka consumer groups; dead-letter alert for processing failures. Tradeoff: Database-per-service requires cross-service queries (e.g., permit status dashboard) to compose results from multiple API calls; handled by permit-service aggregation endpoint that fans out to review and inspection services. Tradeoff: Next.js SSR for public permit status pages adds build complexity but enables SEO indexing of permit numbers for title search use cases.
target_output.open_questions_resolved: Confirmed permit-service as the cross-service status aggregate (not a separate orchestration service) to minimize deployment surface. Confirmed Kafka over REST for cross-service status events to decouple service availability. Confirmed Azure Event Hubs Kafka-compatible endpoint to avoid separate Kafka cluster management. Fee schedule engine deferred to Phase 2; Phase 1 uses static lookup table by permit type.

metadata.notes: City building department permitting portal with four microservices and Kafka event bus for permit lifecycle orchestration; plan_quality moderate with strong microservices design but fee calculation left partially unresolved.

profile.domain: State Child Welfare - CPS Field Worker Mobile Application

project_goal.value: Build a native mobile application for state child protective services field workers to document home visit observations, safety assessments, and case plan progress in the field, syncing with the state SACWIS child welfare information system when connectivity is restored.
project_goal.rationale: Field workers currently use paper forms during visits and re-enter data at the office; mobile app eliminates double-entry and reduces federal timeliness compliance failures.

target_users.value: CPS field workers conducting home visits and safety assessments, supervisors reviewing submitted visit documentation, and the state SACWIS integration consuming structured assessment data.
target_users.rationale: Three personas identified in CPS workflow analysis.

project_class.rationale: Primary interaction is a Flutter mobile app for field workers with offline-first data capture; backend provides SACWIS sync API.

capabilities.value: frontend, backend, data, auth, integrations, batch_jobs, devops
capabilities.rationale: Flutter frontend, Spring Boot backend, SACWIS integration, batch sync jobs, no analytics or payments.

complexity_level.rationale: Offline-first sync with conflict resolution, SACWIS HL7-style integration, and Title IV timeliness tracking across concurrent case records.

risk_level.rationale: Handles highly confidential child welfare case records; CJIS security policy applies due to law enforcement integration; federal Title IV-E compliance.

data_sensitivity.rationale: Child welfare case records are among the most sensitive state government records; state confidentiality statutes and CAPTA restrict access.

external_exposure.rationale: App available only to authenticated, provisioned CPS field workers; no public access.

access_model.value: Keycloak with MFA. Field worker device must be provisioned via MDM (Jamf Pro). Roles: field_worker (access to own assigned cases only), supervisor (access to team cases and review queue), system_admin (user provisioning, no case data access).
access_model.rationale: CJIS policy requires MFA and device management for access to systems containing criminal justice information.

feature_scope.value: Offline home visit documentation with structured safety assessment forms, case plan progress recording, offline photo capture for evidence, automatic SACWIS sync on connectivity restoration, supervisor review and approval of submitted visits, federal timeliness deadline tracking, court hearing calendar integration.
feature_scope.rationale: Covers full field worker documentation workflow replacing paper-based process.

mvp_scope.value: Offline safety assessment forms, home visit documentation, SACWIS sync for new assessments, Keycloak MFA, MDM-required device provisioning.
mvp_scope.rationale: Core field documentation and sync deliver immediate compliance benefit.

security_baseline.value: AES-256 encryption for all data at rest on device (Flutter secure storage). TLS 1.3 in transit. Keycloak MFA required. Remote wipe via MDM on device loss. Session timeout after 15 minutes of inactivity. No data on device beyond current worker's assigned cases.
security_baseline.rationale: CJIS security policy minimum controls for mobile devices accessing case data.

frontend_stack.value: Flutter with Dart, targeting iOS 16+ and Android 12+.
frontend_stack.rationale: Single codebase for both state-issued iOS and Android devices; Dart's strong typing reduces form validation errors in complex assessment data entry.

backend_stack.value: Java 21 with Spring Boot 3 on AWS GovCloud ECS Fargate.
backend_stack.rationale: Java ecosystem for SACWIS integration libraries; Spring Boot for REST API with Keycloak adapter.

data_platform.value: PostgreSQL 16 on AWS GovCloud RDS Multi-AZ. SQLite via Drift on device for offline storage.
data_platform.rationale: PostgreSQL for server-side case records with RDS managed HA; Drift (SQLite) for structured offline data capture with type-safe Dart query layer.

hosting_target.value: AWS GovCloud (us-gov-west-1) ECS Fargate for API, RDS Multi-AZ for database.
hosting_target.rationale: GovCloud required for federal child welfare data under Title IV-E; FedRAMP Moderate hosting environment.

privacy_retention_policy.value: Case records retained per state child welfare records schedule (minimum 10 years or until child turns 28, whichever is later). On-device data cleared when case is closed or worker is reassigned. Audit logs retained 7 years.
privacy_retention_policy.rationale: State confidentiality statute and federal CAPTA requirements.

future_scope.value: Real-time supervisor notifications on high-risk safety assessment scores. Integration with state court case management system for hearing outcomes. Risk scoring model using assessment history.
future_scope.rationale: Phase 3 features pending data governance approval for predictive model use.

constraints.value: SACWIS integration must use the state's existing XML/REST batch sync interface (no SACWIS schema changes). Field workers operate in rural areas with intermittent connectivity; offline must function for full 8-hour shift. All servers must reside in AWS GovCloud. MDM enrollment required before app installation.
constraints.rationale: SACWIS is a legacy system with a defined integration interface; GovCloud is the approved FedRAMP environment.

observability_baseline.value: Datadog APM on ECS Fargate. Structured JSON logs with case_id (hashed) and worker_id. Alert on SACWIS sync failure rate > 5% or sync queue backlog > 500 records. No full PII in log lines.
observability_baseline.rationale: Sync failures directly impact federal compliance reporting; proactive alerting prevents silent data loss.

execution_preference.value: Two-week Agile sprints with field worker UAT sessions every 4 sprints.
execution_preference.rationale: Field worker feedback on form usability is critical; quarterly UAT validates real-world offline behavior.

compliance_context.value: Title IV-B and IV-E federal child welfare regulations (45 CFR Part 1355). CAPTA (Child Abuse Prevention and Treatment Act) data security requirements. State child welfare confidentiality statutes. CJIS Security Policy v5.9 for mobile devices. FedRAMP Moderate for AWS GovCloud hosting.
compliance_context.rationale: Federal funding for child welfare services requires compliance with Title IV federal regulations and CJIS policy for any system used by workers with access to criminal justice information.

project.goal: Offline-first Flutter mobile app for CPS field documentation with SACWIS sync
project.target_users: CPS field workers, supervisors, SACWIS integration

frontend.offline_storage: Drift (SQLite) on device with AES-256 encryption via flutter_secure_storage
frontend.state: Riverpod for reactive state management and offline/online sync state

backend.framework: Spring Boot 3 with Spring Security + Keycloak adapter

security.auth: Keycloak MFA with CJIS-compliant session controls
security.device: MDM-provisioned devices only; remote wipe capability

data.sync: Pull-on-connect, push-on-queue with last-write-wins + supervisor conflict override

reasoner_reviews.product: Round 4 must close the remaining ISS-002 and ISS-003 issues. The SACWIS conflict resolution for concurrent edits has been underspecified across all three prior rounds; this is the most critical gap because concurrent supervisor-and-worker edits to the same case record during sync are a real operational scenario that the current plan does not resolve. The supervisor review approval workflow is also still thin.
reasoner_reviews.architect_reasoner: The Drift + PostgreSQL sync model is correct for offline-first. The unresolved gap is the conflict resolution strategy: the plan has mentioned 'last-write-wins' as a placeholder but not described how the server detects concurrent edits (device_version_vector or updated_at timestamp comparison) or how conflicts are surfaced to supervisors for manual resolution. This must be fully specified in round 4.
reasoner_reviews.security: CJIS compliance requires that access to case records on device is scoped strictly to the assigned worker's cases. The plan must specify how the sync endpoint enforces this: the server must filter records returned to a given worker_id and reject pushes for case_ids not in the worker's active assignment list. This was flagged in ISS-004 but not addressed.
reasoner_reviews.constraints: The SACWIS XML batch interface constraint means the sync pipeline involves a transformation step: internal JSON case records must be serialized to the legacy SACWIS XML schema before batch submission. This transformation layer has not been described architecturally.

backend.service_design: Spring Boot monolith with service packages: cases (case record CRUD, assignment management), assessments (safety assessment schema, scoring), visits (home visit records), sync (device sync protocol, conflict detection), sacwis_integration (SACWIS XML transformation and batch submission), notifications (supervisor alert dispatch).
backend.api_patterns: REST API: authenticated endpoints scoped by worker_id JWT claim. GET /api/v1/sync/pull?worker_id=&since=&limit= returns case records delta for worker. POST /api/v1/sync/push accepts batch of assessment/visit records from device. GET /api/v1/cases?worker_id=&status= for supervisor queue. SACWIS sync: internal batch job not exposed as API.
backend.failure_handling: SACWIS batch submission with retry queue: failed XML submissions stored in sacwis_retry_queue with retry_count and next_retry_at (exponential backoff). Alert on retry_count > 3. Sync push validation: server rejects records with case_id not in worker's active assignments; rejected records returned in push response with error_code UNAUTHORIZED_CASE.
backend.background_jobs: Scheduled Spring @Scheduled jobs: sacwis_batch_submit runs every 30 minutes extracting approved assessments from pending_sacwis_queue, transforms to XML, POSTs to SACWIS batch endpoint. federal_timeliness_check runs nightly scanning case_records for 45 CFR Part 1355 deadline violations (initial assessment within 24h of report, subsequent visits per case plan), creating timeliness_alerts for supervisor review.

frontend.app_structure: Flutter app with feature directories: cases (case list, case detail), assessments (safety assessment form, scoring display), visits (home visit form, photo capture), sync (sync status widget, conflict resolution screen), auth (Keycloak login, biometric unlock after initial auth). Riverpod providers for sync state machine, case data cache, and connectivity status.
frontend.pages_and_flows: Case List: assigned cases with risk level indicator, sync status badge, upcoming visit deadline. Case Detail: family members, assessment history, case plan goals. Safety Assessment Form: structured yes/no/narrative fields for 12 safety factor domains (caregiver functioning, child vulnerability, household hazards, etc.) with aggregate risk score calculation. Home Visit Form: narrative entry, observation checkboxes, photo capture with SQLite blob storage. Sync Status screen: pending records count, last sync timestamp, conflict resolution queue. Conflict Resolution Screen: side-by-side diff of device version vs server version with supervisor override option.
frontend.state_management: Riverpod: CasesNotifier (device Drift query, refresh on sync), SyncStateNotifier (pending push queue, sync in progress flag, conflict list), ConnectivityNotifier (network state via connectivity_plus package). All forms write to Drift immediately; SyncStateNotifier batches pending records and pushes on connectivity restore.

security.auth_design: Keycloak realm for CPS mobile app. Initial auth requires username/password + TOTP MFA. JWT access token (15-minute TTL), refresh token (8-hour TTL matching shift length) stored in flutter_secure_storage. Biometric unlock (Face ID/fingerprint) re-issues access token using stored refresh token without re-entering MFA after initial authentication. Session expires after 8-hour refresh token TTL; full re-auth required.
security.authorization_model: JWT claims: worker_id, roles[field_worker/supervisor/system_admin], district_id. Server enforces: field_worker can pull/push only cases where assigned_worker_id = worker_id JWT claim. Supervisor can view cases where supervisor_id = worker_id claim. case_id authorization check on every sync push record before write.
security.privacy_controls: On-device: Drift database file encrypted via SQLCipher with key derived from flutter_secure_storage. Case records synced to device include only cases in worker's active_assignments. On case closure or worker reassignment, server pushes device_wipe_command in next sync pull response; Flutter app deletes Drift database file and clears secure storage. Remote wipe via Jamf MDM as backup.
security.incident_response_notes: Lost or stolen device: supervisor initiates remote wipe via Jamf MDM console; Keycloak admin revokes refresh token for worker; next sync attempt fails auth, triggering device-side data clear. CJIS incident reporting: any confirmed unauthorized access to device triggers mandatory CJIS incident report within 24 hours.

data.entities: Server DB (PostgreSQL): case_records (id UUID PK, case_number VARCHAR, child_id FK, assigned_worker_id FK, supervisor_id FK, case_status ENUM open/closed/transferred, opened_at, closed_at, version_vector JSONB). family_members (id UUID PK, case_id FK, name_encrypted, dob_encrypted, relationship ENUM child/parent/guardian/sibling, role ENUM subject_child/household_member). home_visits (id UUID PK, case_id FK, worker_id FK, visit_date DATE, visit_type ENUM announced/unannounced/virtual, observations_text ENCRYPTED, photos_s3_keys TEXT[], submitted_at, sync_source ENUM device/web, sacwis_submitted BOOL). safety_assessments (id UUID PK, case_id FK, worker_id FK, assessment_date DATE, safety_factors JSONB (12 domain scores), overall_risk ENUM safe/unsafe/conditionally_safe, narrative_encrypted TEXT, version INT, device_updated_at TIMESTAMPTZ, server_updated_at TIMESTAMPTZ, conflict_status ENUM none/pending_review/resolved). Device DB (SQLite via Drift): pending_push_queue (local_id, record_type ENUM visit/assessment, payload_json, created_at, push_attempts INT). cached_cases (case_id, last_synced_at, payload_json). Sync conflict record: when server detects device_updated_at < server_updated_at for same case_id and assessment_date, creates conflict_record with device_payload and server_payload; supervisor resolves via side-by-side screen.
data.retention_and_deletion: Case records retained per state schedule (10 years or until child age 28). On-device data cleared on case closure via server-pushed device_wipe_command. Encrypted backups of PostgreSQL RDS via automated snapshots, retained 35 days.
data.migration_notes: Historical paper-based visit records imported via CSV extraction from legacy case management system. Assessment records not migrated; only open cases migrated with current status and assignment data.

devops.deployment_topology: Jenkins on GovCloud EC2 -> Docker build -> ECR push -> ECS Fargate task definition update -> ALB health check -> traffic shift. RDS PostgreSQL Multi-AZ in us-gov-west-1. ALB restricted to state VPN CIDR; no public internet access to API.
devops.ci_cd_design: Jenkins pipeline: unit tests (JUnit, Mockito), integration tests against Testcontainers PostgreSQL, docker build, push to ECR, ECS deploy with rolling update. Flutter CI: flutter test, flutter build apk --release, fastlane for MDM distribution via Jamf Pro.
devops.backup_and_recovery: RDS automated snapshots every 6 hours, retained 35 days. Point-in-time recovery enabled. ECS Fargate stateless; no persistent volumes to back up.
devops.devops_risks: Jenkins EC2 in GovCloud requires FedRAMP boundary documentation for CI/CD pipeline. MDM-gated app distribution via Jamf means app updates require device to be online during MDM policy sync; workers with extended field absence may run outdated app versions.

ISS-001.title: SACWIS XML transformation layer unspecified
ISS-001.detail: Rounds 1-2 mentioned SACWIS sync without describing the JSON-to-XML transformation step required by the legacy batch interface. Round 3 added sacwis_integration package with transformer class.

ISS-002.title: Concurrent edit conflict resolution not specified
ISS-002.detail: Plan mentions last-write-wins as placeholder but does not describe how server detects concurrent edits (version_vector or timestamp comparison), how conflicts are queued for supervisor review, or how the Flutter conflict resolution UI works.

ISS-003.title: Sync push case_id authorization enforcement unspecified
ISS-003.detail: CJIS requires that workers can only push records for cases in their active assignment list. The plan has not specified how the server validates case_id in push payloads against the worker's JWT-scoped assignment list.

ISS-004.title: Device data deletion on case closure underdefined
ISS-004.detail: Round 3 added device_wipe_command in sync pull response and Jamf MDM remote wipe as backup. Resolution accepted.

focus_issues.title: Concurrent edit conflict resolution not specified
focus_issues.detail: Server conflict detection mechanism and supervisor resolution workflow not described.

focus_issues.title: Sync push case_id authorization enforcement unspecified
focus_issues.detail: Server must validate case_id in push payloads against worker's active assignment list per CJIS requirements.

latest_recommendations: Specify the version_vector or timestamp-based conflict detection mechanism in data_model.
Describe the supervisor conflict resolution screen in the frontend section.
Document the case_id authorization check on sync push endpoint with rejected record response format.
Add CJIS-specific authorization test cases to the Spring integration test suite.
