target_output.thinking_summary: Round 2 resolves ISS-002: the deduplicate subcommand needs a --dedupe-threshold FLOAT flag defaulting to 0.85 with validation enforcing range 0.5-1.0. Also clarifying that the data quality report writes as JSON to the output directory (not just terminal output) as the audit recommended. The adapter architecture fix from ISS-001 is already in the specialist subplans. This is a low-risk CLI tool so the plan quality is appropriately weak - several sections are thin but acceptable for the tool class.

fix_report.action_taken: Added --dedupe-threshold FLOAT option to deduplicate subcommand with default 0.85 and Click parameter validation enforcing range 0.5-1.0; help text explains that lower values merge more aggressively and recommends starting at 0.90 for portals with inconsistent vendor name formatting. Per-portal threshold override also supported via portal YAML config file under dedupe_threshold key.
fix_report.expected_outcome: Researchers can tune deduplication sensitivity per dataset; default of 0.85 matches prior behavior; no breaking change to existing workflows.

target_output.title: Civic Procurement Data CLI - Round 2
target_output.executive_summary: A locally-executed Python Click CLI tool distributed via PyPI for civic data engineers to bulk download, validate, and normalize public contracting data from multiple procurement portals. Three portal adapters (California eProcure, Illinois Comptroller, USASpending.gov) with a registry-based PortalAdapter plugin interface, unified ContractAward Pydantic schema, configurable rapidfuzz vendor deduplication, DuckDB optional local persistence, and CSV/Parquet export. No hosted infrastructure; runs on researcher workstations and CI runners.
target_output.architecture_overview: Click command group 'procurement' with four subcommands: download, validate, deduplicate, export. Portal-specific HTTP logic encapsulated in PortalAdapter subclasses registered in adapters/__init__.py; community adapters installable as separate packages that register via Python entry_points. Pydantic v2 ContractAward model defines the unified schema; adapter-specific field mapping in each adapter class. DuckDB and pyarrow are optional extras via pip install procurement-cli[duckdb,parquet]. All processing runs synchronously per command invocation with asyncio for concurrent portal HTTP calls.
target_output.technology_stack: Python 3.12 (compatible 3.10+). Click for CLI command parsing and option validation. httpx with AsyncClient for portal API calls. Pydantic v2 for ContractAward schema validation and field-level error reporting. rapidfuzz for vendor name fuzzy matching with configurable threshold. DuckDB (optional extra) for local SQL query layer. pyarrow (optional extra) for Parquet write. python-dotenv for .env API key loading. Hatch for build and PyPI packaging. GitHub Actions CI with pytest, mypy strict, ruff.
target_output.functional_feature_map: download --portal {slug} --from {date} --to {date}: fetches ContractAward records from specified portal(s) concurrently, writes to Parquet per portal per fiscal year. validate --input {path}: runs Pydantic schema validation on downloaded records, writes validation_errors.jsonl, exits non-zero if errors found and --fail-on-errors set. deduplicate --input {path} --dedupe-threshold {float, default 0.85}: groups ContractAward records by rapidfuzz token_sort_ratio vendor name match above threshold, resolves canonical vendor name and EIN, writes DeduplicationResult manifest. export --format csv|parquet --output {path}: merges deduplicated records from all portals into combined output file. Data quality report written as data_quality_{timestamp}.json to output directory after each download run.
target_output.system_components: Click CLI entry point (procurement) with command group routing. PortalAdapter abstract base class (fetch_awards -> Iterator[RawAwardRecord], get_metadata -> PortalMetadata). Adapter registry dict in adapters/__init__.py mapping portal slug to class. CaliforneaProcureAdapter, IllinoisComptrollerAdapter, USASpendingAdapter: portal-specific httpx pagination and field mapping. SchemaValidator: Pydantic v2 model_validate with error collection mode. VendorDeduplicator: rapidfuzz token_sort_ratio with configurable threshold and EIN resolution pass. DuckDBStore (optional): creates procurement.duckdb and registers Parquet files as views. ExportWriter: Parquet via pyarrow or CSV via csv.DictWriter. DataQualityReporter: generates data_quality_{timestamp}.json with per-portal statistics.
target_output.workflows: Download Workflow: researcher runs 'procurement download --portal ca_eprocure --portal usaspending --from 2024-01-01 --to 2024-12-31'; CLI loads adapter classes from registry; asyncio.run() concurrently calls each adapter's fetch_awards(); each adapter paginates its portal API with httpx AsyncClient, normalizing response JSON to RawAwardRecord; records yielded to SchemaValidator; valid records written to Parquet, invalid to validation_errors.jsonl; DataQualityReporter writes data_quality_{timestamp}.json. Deduplication Workflow: researcher runs 'procurement deduplicate --input output/ --dedupe-threshold 0.90'; VendorDeduplicator loads all ContractAward Parquet files, groups by portal_slug, applies rapidfuzz token_sort_ratio at threshold 0.90 to cluster vendor names, resolves canonical name (most frequent variant) and EIN (any non-null EIN in cluster), writes DeduplicationResult manifest.
target_output.data_model: ContractAward (Pydantic v2 BaseModel): award_id: str, portal_slug: str, vendor_name: str, vendor_ein: str | None, award_amount: Decimal, award_date: date, contract_description: str, agency_name: str, agency_code: str | None, fiscal_year: int, solicitation_type: str | None, normalized_at: datetime. Parquet output schema matches Pydantic field names; Decimal serialized as float64. DeduplicationResult: canonical_vendor_name: str, matched_names: list[str], ein_resolved: str | None, confidence_score: float, record_count: int. DataQualityReport: portal_slug: str, records_fetched: int, records_valid: int, records_invalid: int, top_validation_errors: dict[str, int] (field name to error count).
target_output.api_design: No exposed HTTP API. Portal APIs consumed by adapters: California eProcure GET /api/v2/contracts?cursor={cursor}&page_size=500. Illinois Comptroller GET /api/contracts?offset={n}&limit=1000&modified_after={date}. USASpending.gov POST /api/v2/search/spending_by_award/ with after_cursor pagination. All adapters use httpx AsyncClient with HTTPS verification enabled; Authorization header (if API key set) suppressed in DEBUG log output via httpx event hook.
target_output.security_and_compliance: API keys: loaded from PROCUREMENT_CLI_{PORTAL}_API_KEY environment variable or .env file via python-dotenv; never printed to stdout/stderr; httpx transport event hook redacts Authorization headers before logging at DEBUG level. HTTPS: all portal HTTP calls use httpx default certificate verification; no --no-verify flag exposed. Portal terms of service compliance: README documents each portal's acceptable use policy and rate limit requirements; CLI defaults respect rate limits with configurable --rate-limit-delay. No PII or sensitive data handled; all source data is publicly available government procurement records.
target_output.deployment_and_operations: PyPI distribution: Hatch build produces wheel and sdist; published via GitHub Actions trusted publisher workflow on git tag push. Package extras: [duckdb] adds duckdb>=0.10, [parquet] adds pyarrow>=15. Base install under 20MB verified in CI via wheel size check. GitHub Actions CI matrix: Python 3.10, 3.11, 3.12 on ubuntu-latest and macos-latest; pytest with 80% coverage, mypy strict, ruff, wheel size check, integration tests against portal API mocks using httpx MockTransport. Community adapter contributions: PortalAdapter entry_point registration documented in CONTRIBUTING.md.
target_output.observability: Structured logging to stderr via Python logging module; --verbose flag sets DEBUG level showing per-page fetch progress and validation error samples. Progress bars via Rich Live for multi-portal concurrent download showing records fetched per portal. Data quality report (data_quality_{timestamp}.json) written after each download: per-portal records_fetched, records_valid, records_invalid, top_validation_errors by field. Exit code: 0 on success; 1 on any validation errors when --fail-on-errors flag set; 2 on unrecoverable API errors. CI integration: non-zero exit codes enable automated procurement monitoring pipelines to detect data source changes.
target_output.cost_and_scaling: No infrastructure cost; runs on researcher hardware. Package install time under 30 seconds on standard CI runners. For large portal datasets (100K+ awards): Parquet write via pyarrow streams records without loading all into memory. DuckDB query over Parquet files handles multi-million-row datasets on a laptop. Concurrent portal downloads bounded by --max-concurrent (default 3) to avoid rate limit violations.
target_output.phased_implementation: Phase 1 (8 weeks, complete): three portal adapters, ContractAward Pydantic schema, CLI command group, Parquet and CSV export, basic data quality report, PyPI release. Phase 2 (current round): PortalAdapter plugin interface with entry_points (ISS-001 resolved), configurable --dedupe-threshold CLI flag (ISS-002 resolved), JSON data quality report file, per-portal YAML configuration for threshold overrides. Phase 3: additional state portal adapters from community contributions, OCDS schema export mode, streaming download for multi-gigabyte portal bulk files.
target_output.development_guidelines: Python 3.10+ compatibility; no C extension dependencies in base install. All portal adapters must implement PortalAdapter abstract base class; no portal-specific logic in core CLI modules. Fuzzy match threshold must be passed as parameter to VendorDeduplicator; no module-level constants for threshold values. pytest fixtures for portal API mocks use httpx MockTransport; no real portal API calls in CI. mypy strict mode; all public functions annotated. ruff with isort profile for imports. Semantic versioning; CHANGELOG.md updated on each release.
target_output.risks_and_tradeoffs: Risk: procurement portal APIs change their schema or pagination format without notice, breaking adapters silently. Mitigation: each adapter includes a schema_version field in PortalMetadata; CI integration test against mock responses pinned to known-good portal API responses; adapter tests fail on schema drift. Risk: vendor name fuzzy matching at default 0.85 threshold may incorrectly merge distinct vendors with similar names (e.g., 'Smith Construction LLC' and 'Smith Construction Inc'). Mitigation: configurable --dedupe-threshold with documentation recommending 0.90 for portals with inconsistent formatting; DeduplicationResult manifest lets researchers audit clusters before accepting. Tradeoff: asyncio for concurrent portal downloads adds complexity over sequential requests; justified by 3-5x speedup when downloading from multiple portals in a single command.
target_output.open_questions_resolved: Confirmed --dedupe-threshold flag with default 0.85 and range 0.5-1.0 validation as the ISS-002 resolution. Chose rapidfuzz token_sort_ratio over fuzz.ratio for vendor name matching to handle word order variations (e.g., 'ACME Corp' vs. 'Corp ACME'). Selected pyproject.toml extras_require pattern for DuckDB and pyarrow as optional dependencies. Decided data quality report writes to JSON file rather than only terminal output to support CI pipeline integration.

metadata.notes: Round 2 CLI tool revision; ISS-002 dedupe threshold configurability resolved; plan quality weak reflecting appropriately thin sections for a simple local CLI tool.

profile.domain: Transportation / Government Cloud Infrastructure

project_goal.value: Migrate 40+ on-premise legacy applications and data stores from the state department of transportation data center to a FedRAMP-authorized AWS GovCloud environment, establishing a secure landing zone with centralized network controls, shared services, and a compliance baseline meeting FISMA Moderate requirements
project_goal.rationale: On-premise data center lease expires in 18 months; cloud migration required to avoid hardware refresh costs and establish modern DevOps capabilities for 40+ SDOT applications

target_users.value: State DOT application teams (40+ workloads), department IT security officers, cloud operations team, and FISMA system owners
target_users.rationale: Primary consumers are application teams migrating workloads and operations staff managing the shared landing zone

project_class.rationale: Project delivers platform infrastructure - landing zone, network topology, shared services, compliance controls - not an application with end-user features

capabilities.rationale: Infrastructure automation (Terraform, Ansible), shared data platform services (RDS, S3), and backend runtime environments; no end-user frontend

complexity_level.rationale: 40+ workloads across multiple AWS accounts with centralized network controls, FedRAMP compliance baseline, migration wave orchestration, and cross-account IAM governance

risk_level.rationale: Migration of production transportation systems including traffic management and bridge monitoring; service disruptions could affect public safety

data_sensitivity.rationale: DOT systems include law enforcement-sensitive traffic camera data, bridge structural monitoring data, and internal financial systems requiring FISMA Moderate controls

external_exposure.rationale: Landing zone infrastructure is internal to the state government network; public-facing DOT applications will maintain their existing external exposure profiles post-migration

access_model.value: AWS IAM Identity Center (SSO) with Active Directory federation; least-privilege IAM roles per AWS account and per application team; break-glass emergency access accounts with mandatory CloudTrail logging
access_model.rationale: Centralized identity management required for 40+ workloads across multiple AWS accounts; AD federation preserves department SSO workflow

feature_scope.value: Multi-account AWS Organizations structure, transit gateway network hub, centralized logging account, security tooling account, shared services VPC (DNS, patch management, monitoring), IAM Identity Center with AD federation, AWS Config rules for NIST SP 800-53 controls, migration wave tooling, application team onboarding runbooks
feature_scope.rationale: Covers full landing zone scope required before any application team migrations can begin

mvp_scope.value: Core landing zone with 3 organizational units (Security, Infrastructure, Workloads), Transit Gateway hub-and-spoke network, centralized logging to Security account, IAM Identity Center with AD federation, AWS Config FedRAMP Moderate baseline controls, and successful pilot migration of 2 non-production workloads
mvp_scope.rationale: Landing zone must be proven with pilot workloads before large-scale application team migrations begin

security_baseline.value: AWS Organizations SCPs enforcing GovCloud region restriction and disabling public S3 buckets; GuardDuty enabled in all accounts with findings forwarded to Security account; AWS Config rules for all NIST SP 800-53 Rev 5 Moderate controls; CloudTrail organization trail to centralized logging bucket; Security Hub with FSBP and NIST 800-53 standards enabled
security_baseline.rationale: FedRAMP Moderate ATO requires comprehensive AWS native security tooling baseline

frontend_stack.value: None - infrastructure project with no application UI; Terraform and Ansible are the primary tooling
frontend_stack.rationale: infrastructure_project class; no frontend specialist applicable

backend_stack.value: Terraform 1.8 for all AWS infrastructure provisioning; Ansible for OS-level configuration management on EC2 instances; Python 3.12 scripts for migration automation and compliance reporting
backend_stack.rationale: Terraform is the department IaC standard; Ansible handles instance configuration not manageable by Terraform; Python for custom migration tooling

data_platform.value: Shared RDS PostgreSQL 16 cluster on Multi-AZ for application teams requiring managed relational databases; S3 with Object Lock for immutable audit log and compliance evidence storage; no central OLAP platform in landing zone scope
data_platform.rationale: Shared database platform reduces per-application-team database management overhead; S3 Object Lock ensures audit evidence immutability for FedRAMP

hosting_target.value: AWS GovCloud (us-gov-west-1 and us-gov-east-1 for DR); multi-account structure via AWS Organizations; EKS for containerized workloads migrating from bare-metal Kubernetes
hosting_target.rationale: GovCloud required for FISMA/FedRAMP; dual-region for DR capabilities required by department continuity plan; EKS as the Kubernetes landing zone for containerized applications

privacy_retention_policy.value: CloudTrail organization trail retained 7 years in S3 Glacier Deep Archive; VPC Flow Logs retained 2 years; AWS Config snapshots retained 3 years; application data retention policies delegated to individual system owners per FISMA system boundary documentation
privacy_retention_policy.rationale: Federal records retention requirements for FISMA audit evidence; application data retention governed by individual SSPs

future_scope.value: AWS Service Catalog for self-service application team vending, Control Tower for account factory automation, Service Mesh (App Mesh or Istio) for EKS workloads, cross-agency data sharing via AWS Resource Access Manager, FinOps cost allocation dashboards
future_scope.rationale: Post-landing-zone capabilities to increase application team autonomy and improve cost visibility

constraints.value: 18-month data center lease deadline for all 40+ workload migrations; FedRAMP Moderate ATO required before any production traffic migrates; some legacy applications have mainframe dependencies that cannot be containerized; existing application team Terraform skill level varies significantly
constraints.rationale: Hard deadline driven by lease expiry; ATO required for regulatory compliance; mainframe-dependent apps require special migration strategy; skills gap requires onboarding program

observability_baseline.value: Datadog agent on all EC2 instances and EKS node groups; centralized CloudWatch log aggregation to logging account; Security Hub compliance dashboard in security account; Grafana dashboards for landing zone infrastructure health in shared services account
observability_baseline.rationale: Department IT monitoring standard is Datadog; Security Hub provides FedRAMP compliance posture dashboard

execution_preference.value: GitLab CI for Terraform and Ansible pipelines with plan/apply split requiring human approval for production changes; Terraform state in S3 with DynamoDB locking per account; module-based Terraform architecture for reuse across application team accounts
execution_preference.rationale: GitLab is the department standard; plan/apply split prevents accidental production changes; module reuse reduces per-team landing zone onboarding effort

compliance_context.value: FedRAMP Moderate authorization required; FISMA Moderate impact baseline; NIST SP 800-53 Rev 5 control families: AC, AU, CA, CM, IA, IR, RA, SC, SI; state IT security standards; department data governance policy for transportation operational data
compliance_context.rationale: risk_level=high and data_sensitivity=confidential trigger mandatory compliance context

project.goal: FedRAMP-authorized AWS GovCloud landing zone for 40+ state DOT workload migration
project.target_users: DOT application teams, IT security officers, cloud ops
project.timeline: Landing zone complete in 6 months; all migrations complete within 18 months

backend.automation: Python 3.12 migration scripts and compliance reporting

security.identity: IAM Identity Center with Active Directory federation
security.guardrails: AWS Organizations SCPs for region restriction and public S3 disable
security.compliance: AWS Config + Security Hub NIST 800-53 Moderate standard

data.shared_db: RDS PostgreSQL 16 Multi-AZ for application teams
data.audit_storage: S3 with Object Lock for CloudTrail and compliance evidence

devops.environments: landing zone, workload accounts per application team

constraints.mainframe_apps: Cannot containerize; require special migration path

open_questions.ate_timeline: FedRAMP ATO timeline with agency sponsor TBD

confirmed_decisions.identity: IAM Identity Center with AD federation

reasoner_reviews.product: The landing zone scope correctly covers the foundational infrastructure required before any application migrations. The 18-month deadline with 40+ workloads creates urgency for early landing zone completion and a well-structured migration wave plan. The pilot migration of 2 non-production workloads is the right validation approach before committing application teams to the migration process. The 3 mainframe-dependent applications need separate discovery sprints as they may not complete within the 18-month window.
reasoner_reviews.architect_reasoner: The multi-account AWS Organizations structure with Transit Gateway hub-and-spoke is the correct network topology for this scale. Recommend organizing accounts into 4 OUs: Security (centralized logging and security tooling), Infrastructure (shared services, transit, DNS), Workloads (production app team accounts), and Sandbox (dev/test). The Terraform module architecture is critical - a landing zone module, account vending module, and workload baseline module will significantly reduce per-team onboarding effort. EKS cluster should be a shared multi-tenant cluster in the infrastructure account rather than per-application-team clusters to control costs.
reasoner_reviews.security: FedRAMP Moderate ATO requires a System Security Plan covering all 325 NIST SP 800-53 Rev 5 Moderate controls. AWS inherits a significant number of these controls for GovCloud services, but the landing zone team must document the customer-responsible controls and provide continuous monitoring evidence. The SCP guardrails must include region restriction to us-gov-west-1 and us-gov-east-1 only, public S3 bucket prevention, require EC2 instance termination protection on tagged production instances, and prevent IAM user creation without MFA. AWS Config custom rules will be needed for DOT-specific controls not covered by FSBP.
reasoner_reviews.constraints: 18 months for 40+ workloads is aggressive but achievable with structured migration waves and self-service tooling. Recommend a 5-wave migration plan organized by risk and complexity: Wave 1 (development environments), Wave 2 (non-critical production), Wave 3 (standard production), Wave 4 (high-criticality production), Wave 5 (mainframe-adjacent applications with special handling). Application teams with low Terraform proficiency will need an embedded cloud engineer for Wave 2-3 migrations.

backend.service_design: Terraform module library structure in GitLab: modules/landing-zone (AWS Organizations, IAM Identity Center, CloudTrail org trail, GuardDuty org, Security Hub org), modules/network (Transit Gateway, hub VPC, spoke VPC, DNS resolver rules, VPC Flow Logs), modules/account-baseline (Config rules, IAM roles, Datadog agent SSM association, patch baseline), modules/shared-services (RDS shared cluster, S3 logging buckets, Route53 private zones), modules/eks-cluster (shared EKS 1.29 cluster with Karpenter node provisioner). Application team accounts instantiate account-baseline module plus workload-specific resources.
backend.business_modules: Migration wave orchestration: Python script building dependency graph from application inventory YAML, generating ordered migration cohort lists respecting inter-app dependencies. Compliance evidence collector: Python Lambda running nightly, querying AWS Config, Security Hub, and CloudTrail to generate POA&M-ready compliance reports per FISMA system boundary. Application team onboarding automation: account vending pipeline creating new AWS account, applying account-baseline module, configuring IAM Identity Center assignments, and generating team-specific Terraform starter template.
backend.failure_handling: Terraform plan validation in GitLab CI with tfsec static analysis and Checkov policy scanning before apply stage. Production Terraform apply requires explicit approval in GitLab pipeline with 24-hour expiry. S3 + DynamoDB state locking prevents concurrent applies. Automated drift detection: scheduled GitLab pipeline runs terraform plan in check mode weekly; PagerDuty alert on detected drift. Rollback runbooks for each migration wave stored in GitLab wiki.

security.auth_design: IAM Identity Center with AD SAML 2.0 federation; permission sets mapped to AD security groups (CloudAdmins, SecurityAuditors, AppTeamDevs, AppTeamOps, ReadOnly); permission sets scoped to specific account assignments preventing cross-account privilege escalation. Break-glass accounts in each account with MFA hardware token requirement; access activations logged to Security Hub and PagerDuty.
security.authorization_model: AWS Organizations SCPs as guardrails: DenyCrossRegion (blocks all API calls outside us-gov-west-1 and us-gov-east-1), DenyPublicS3 (blocks s3:PutBucketAcl public grants and s3:PutBucketPublicAccessBlock false), DenyRootUserActions (blocks root API usage outside break-glass procedure), RequireIMDSv2 (blocks EC2 launch without IMDSv2 metadata service enforcement). Application-specific IAM roles provisioned by account-baseline module with least-privilege policies reviewed by security team.
security.audit_and_logging_controls: CloudTrail organization trail writing management and data events to centralized S3 bucket in logging account with Object Lock COMPLIANCE mode (7-year retention). VPC Flow Logs enabled for all VPCs, aggregated to logging account. Security Hub findings forwarded to SIEM via EventBridge. AWS Config continuous recording in all accounts with configuration history in logging account S3. Monthly compliance evidence reports generated by Python Lambda from Config and Security Hub data for FedRAMP continuous monitoring.

data.entities: landing_zones (account_id, account_name, ou_name, owner_team, migration_wave, baseline_applied_at, compliance_score). migration_waves (wave_id, wave_name, target_date, applications JSONB, status ENUM planned/in_progress/complete). application_inventories (app_id, app_name, owner_team, on_prem_server, dependencies JSONB, migration_strategy ENUM lift_and_shift/replatform/refactor/retire, estimated_wave, migration_status). security_controls (control_id NIST_SP_800-53, control_family, implementation_status ENUM inherited/shared/customer, evidence_s3_uri, last_assessed_at). compliance_baselines (account_id, config_rule_name, compliance_status, last_evaluated_at).
data.storage_design: Terraform state S3 buckets: one per AWS account with DynamoDB locking table, versioning enabled, MFA delete enabled. CloudTrail bucket in logging account: S3 Object Lock COMPLIANCE mode, 7-year retention, KMS CMK encryption. Config snapshots bucket in logging account: S3 Intelligent-Tiering, 3-year retention. Shared services: S3 buckets for application team use with bucket policy templates enforcing encryption and versioning.

devops.deployment_topology: AWS Organizations with 4 OUs: Security OU (logging account, security-tooling account), Infrastructure OU (network account with Transit Gateway, shared-services account with EKS and RDS), Workloads OU (one account per application team in production, one per team in non-prod), Sandbox OU (unrestricted dev accounts for experimentation). Transit Gateway hub in network account with VPC attachments from all spoke accounts. Direct Connect or Site-to-Site VPN from on-premise data center during migration period.
devops.ci_cd_design: GitLab CI pipeline stages: terraform fmt check, tfsec security scan, Checkov policy scan (block on HIGH findings), terraform validate, terraform plan (output posted to MR), manual approval gate for production applies, terraform apply, post-apply drift check via plan -detailed-exitcode. Separate pipelines for landing zone core (security, network, shared-services accounts - change-controlled) and application team accounts (standard approval flow). Ansible playbooks in separate repo with molecule testing.
devops.observability_stack: Datadog infrastructure monitoring: EC2 instance health, EKS cluster node and pod metrics, RDS connection counts and replication lag. Security Hub compliance dashboard in security account: FSBP and NIST 800-53 scores per account. Grafana in shared-services account: Transit Gateway flow metrics, Config compliance trends, migration wave progress. PagerDuty: Terraform drift detection alerts, Security Hub critical findings, GuardDuty high-severity findings.

target_output.thinking_summary: Designing a FedRAMP Moderate landing zone for 40+ state DOT workloads on AWS GovCloud. Critical decisions: 4-OU Organizations structure (Security, Infrastructure, Workloads, Sandbox) with Transit Gateway hub-and-spoke network; SCP guardrails enforcing GovCloud region restriction and disabling public S3 buckets across all accounts; Terraform module library with landing-zone, network, account-baseline, and eks-cluster modules enabling self-service application team onboarding; 5-wave migration plan organized by risk and dependency graph. FedRAMP Moderate ATO timeline is the binding constraint - all NIST SP 800-53 Rev 5 Moderate customer-responsible controls must be documented and evidenced before production migrations proceed.
target_output.title: State Department of Transportation Cloud Landing Zone
target_output.executive_summary: A FedRAMP Moderate-compliant AWS GovCloud landing zone for the state department of transportation, establishing a multi-account AWS Organizations structure with centralized network controls via Transit Gateway, identity federation through IAM Identity Center with Active Directory, SCP guardrails enforcing GovCloud region restriction and public S3 prevention, and a continuous compliance baseline via AWS Config and Security Hub against NIST SP 800-53 Rev 5 Moderate controls. All infrastructure provisioned via a modular Terraform library enabling self-service application team account vending. A 5-wave migration plan moves 40+ legacy data center workloads to the landing zone within the 18-month lease expiry deadline, with FedRAMP ATO required before any production traffic migrates.
target_output.architecture_overview: Four-OU AWS Organizations structure: Security OU hosts the centralized logging account (CloudTrail org trail, VPC Flow Logs, Config history) and security-tooling account (Security Hub, GuardDuty, IAM Access Analyzer findings aggregator). Infrastructure OU hosts the network account (Transit Gateway, hub VPC, Direct Connect gateway, Route53 Resolver) and shared-services account (EKS 1.29 shared cluster, RDS PostgreSQL 16 Multi-AZ, shared S3 buckets, Datadog monitoring). Workloads OU contains one production and one non-production AWS account per application team (vended via account-baseline Terraform module). Sandbox OU provides unrestricted development accounts with SCPs relaxed for experimentation. Transit Gateway in the network account creates hub-and-spoke topology with VPC attachments from all spoke accounts routed through centralized inspection via a shared services VPC with AWS Network Firewall. IAM Identity Center federates Active Directory security groups to permission sets scoped per account assignment. On-premise data center connectivity maintained via Site-to-Site VPN during migration, replaced by Direct Connect at completion.
target_output.technology_stack: IaC: Terraform 1.8 with modular architecture; state in S3 + DynamoDB per account. Config management: Ansible 2.16 for EC2 instance OS configuration with Molecule testing. Automation: Python 3.12 for migration orchestration scripts and FISMA compliance evidence collection. Identity: IAM Identity Center with AD SAML 2.0 federation. Network: AWS Transit Gateway hub-and-spoke; AWS Network Firewall for centralized inspection; Route53 Resolver for hybrid DNS. Compliance: AWS Config custom and managed rules; Security Hub with FSBP and NIST 800-53 standards; GuardDuty org enrollment. Containers: EKS 1.29 with Karpenter; ECR per application account. Monitoring: Datadog agent; Grafana on shared-services EKS; PagerDuty.
target_output.functional_feature_map: Landing Zone Core: Organizations multi-account structure, IAM Identity Center AD federation, CloudTrail organization trail, GuardDuty organization, Security Hub organization with NIST 800-53 standard. Network Hub: Transit Gateway with spoke VPC attachments, Network Firewall centralized inspection, hybrid DNS via Route53 Resolver. Shared Services: EKS shared cluster for containerized workloads, RDS PostgreSQL Multi-AZ shared cluster, shared S3 bucket patterns with encryption enforcement. Account Vending: automated Terraform pipeline creating new workload account, applying account-baseline module, configuring IAM Identity Center permission set assignments, generating team starter templates. Migration Tooling: dependency graph builder from application inventory YAML, migration wave cohort generator, pre-migration checklist validator, post-migration smoke test runner. Compliance Automation: nightly Lambda collecting Config and Security Hub evidence for FISMA system boundary POA&M reports.
target_output.system_components: AWS Organizations (management account, 4 OUs, account factory). IAM Identity Center with AD SAML 2.0 connector. Transit Gateway (hub in network account, spoke attachments from all workload VPCs). AWS Network Firewall (centralized inspection VPC in network account). Route53 Resolver (hybrid DNS forwarding between GovCloud and on-premise). Security Hub (FSBP, NIST 800-53 standard, findings aggregation to security-tooling account). GuardDuty organization (threat detection, findings to security-tooling account). AWS Config (organization recorder, managed and custom rules, history to logging account S3). CloudTrail (organization trail, management and data events, S3 Object Lock logging account). EKS 1.29 shared cluster with Karpenter (shared-services account). RDS PostgreSQL 16 Multi-AZ (shared-services account). GitLab CI runners (shared-services account, managed by Ansible). Datadog agent deployment via SSM State Manager association. Terraform state S3 buckets with DynamoDB locking (one per AWS account).
target_output.workflows: Application Team Account Vending: cloud ops engineer creates a new application team entry in accounts.yaml in GitLab; CI pipeline runs account-vending Terraform module in management account to create new AWS account in Workloads OU; account-baseline module applies in new account creating VPC, Transit Gateway attachment, IAM roles, Config rules, Datadog SSM association, and patch baseline; IAM Identity Center permission set assignments created mapping team AD groups to account; team receives onboarding email with Terraform starter template and account IDs. Migration Wave Execution: migration wave lead runs migration-planner.py against application-inventory.yaml to generate ordered cohort for the wave respecting dependency graph; each application team receives migration runbook with pre-migration checklist (connectivity test, credential rotation, backup verification), migration execution steps (AWS Server Migration Service or container re-platforming), and post-migration smoke test scripts; wave lead tracks cohort progress in shared Confluence migration tracker; rollback runbook stored in GitLab wiki per application.
target_output.data_model: application_inventories (app_id UUID PK, app_name VARCHAR, owner_team VARCHAR, on_prem_server VARCHAR, server_os VARCHAR, migration_strategy ENUM lift_and_shift/replatform/refactor/retire, target_account_id VARCHAR, target_wave INT, dependencies JSONB list of app_ids, migration_status ENUM discovery/ready/in_progress/complete/blocked, blockers TEXT, updated_at). migration_waves (wave_id INT PK, wave_name VARCHAR, target_completion DATE, applications JSONB, status ENUM planned/in_progress/complete, actual_completion DATE). landing_zones (account_id VARCHAR PK, account_name, ou_name, owner_team, baseline_module_version, baseline_applied_at, compliance_score FLOAT, last_compliance_check_at). security_controls (control_id VARCHAR NIST_SP_800-53 PK, control_family, control_name, implementation_status ENUM inherited/shared/customer, evidence_s3_uri, assessed_by, last_assessed_at). All stored in SQLite in the cloud ops GitLab project for portability; no separate database required for infrastructure tracking data.
target_output.api_design: No external API surface. Internal tooling interfaces: migration-planner.py CLI (input: application-inventory.yaml, output: wave cohort JSON with dependency ordering). compliance-reporter.py Lambda (triggered nightly, queries Config/Security Hub APIs, outputs FISMA evidence ZIP to S3). account-vending GitLab CI pipeline triggered by accounts.yaml commit (creates account, applies Terraform, sends onboarding email). Terraform module interfaces: modules/account-baseline accepts account_id, team_name, vpc_cidr, transit_gateway_id, datadog_api_key_arn. modules/eks-cluster accepts cluster_version, node_groups JSONB, karpenter_node_classes JSONB.
target_output.security_and_compliance: AWS Organizations SCPs: DenyCrossRegion blocks all API calls outside us-gov-west-1 and us-gov-east-1; DenyPublicS3 blocks public bucket ACLs and public access block disabling; DenyRootUserActions outside break-glass procedure; RequireIMDSv2 blocks EC2 launch without IMDSv2 enforcement; DenyUnencryptedS3Upload blocks S3 puts without SSE-KMS or SSE-S3. FedRAMP Moderate: System Security Plan documents 325 NIST SP 800-53 Rev 5 Moderate controls; AWS inherits approximately 116 controls for GovCloud services; customer-responsible controls documented with implementation descriptions and continuous monitoring evidence from Config and Security Hub. FISMA: CloudTrail organization trail with 7-year Object Lock retention provides audit log immutability evidence. GuardDuty threat detection with findings forwarded to Security Hub and SIEM. Security Hub NIST 800-53 standard enabled in all accounts with consolidated findings in security-tooling account. tfsec and Checkov in CI pipeline block Terraform changes introducing HIGH-severity security findings. Break-glass accounts require hardware MFA token and PagerDuty incident creation for audit trail.
target_output.deployment_and_operations: Landing zone bootstrapping sequence: 1) management account setup (Organizations, IAM Identity Center), 2) Security OU accounts (logging, security-tooling) with CloudTrail and Security Hub org enrollment, 3) Infrastructure OU accounts (network with Transit Gateway, shared-services with EKS and RDS), 4) Direct Connect or VPN connectivity to on-premise, 5) first application team account vending as pilot validation. GitLab CI pipeline for landing zone modules: tfsec scan, Checkov HIGH block, terraform validate, terraform plan (posted to MR comment), manual approval gate, terraform apply, post-apply compliance check. Production landing zone changes require review by cloud ops lead and IT security officer. Application team account pipelines use standard approval flow. Ansible playbooks for EC2 configuration deployed via SSM State Manager run command triggered by CI pipeline on instance launch. Migration wave operations managed via GitLab project kanban with application status tracked in application-inventory.yaml commits.
target_output.observability: Datadog infrastructure dashboards: EC2 instance inventory and health across all workload accounts via Datadog AWS integration, EKS cluster node and pod resource utilization, RDS connection pool and replication lag on shared cluster. Security Hub compliance dashboard: FSBP and NIST 800-53 scores per account trending over time; critical and high findings backlog. Grafana on shared-services EKS: Transit Gateway bytes-in/out and packet drop metrics, Config compliance rule pass/fail counts by account, migration wave completion percentage and velocity. CloudWatch metrics for account-baseline module health: Config recorder enabled status, GuardDuty active status, CloudTrail trail enabled status - alerts on any deviation. PagerDuty: GuardDuty HIGH/CRITICAL findings, Security Hub CRITICAL findings, Terraform drift detected, Transit Gateway packet drops above threshold.
target_output.cost_and_scaling: Estimated infrastructure account monthly costs: Transit Gateway attachment fees for 40+ VPCs approximately $800/month at scale. EKS shared cluster (3 m6i.xlarge nodes base) approximately $600/month; Karpenter scales nodes on workload demand. RDS PostgreSQL Multi-AZ (db.r6g.large) approximately $400/month shared across teams. Security tooling (GuardDuty, Security Hub, Config, CloudTrail) approximately $1,200/month across all accounts at 40 accounts. Datadog infrastructure monitoring approximately $800/month for 100 hosts. Total shared infrastructure approximately $4,000-5,000/month excluding individual application team workload costs. FinOps: AWS Cost Anomaly Detection alerts on account-level spend spikes; resource tagging policy enforced via SCP requiring owner and project tags on all EC2 and RDS resources.
target_output.phased_implementation: Phase 1 Landing Zone Core (12 weeks): management account Organizations setup, Security OU accounts with CloudTrail org trail and Security Hub, Infrastructure OU network account with Transit Gateway and VPN to on-premise data center, IAM Identity Center AD federation, SCP guardrail deployment, FedRAMP SSP documentation initiation. Phase 2 Shared Services and Pilot (6 weeks): shared-services account with EKS and RDS, account-baseline Terraform module finalization, account vending pipeline, migration tooling (dependency graph builder, pre-migration checklist), pilot migration of 2 non-production workloads, FedRAMP ATO process with agency sponsor. Phase 3 Wave 1-2 Migrations (4 months): development environment migrations (Wave 1, ~15 workloads), non-critical production migrations (Wave 2, ~10 workloads), application team onboarding program for teams with low Terraform proficiency. Phase 4 Wave 3-4 Migrations (4 months): standard production workloads (Wave 3, ~12 workloads), high-criticality production systems including traffic management (Wave 4, ~5 workloads) with cutover windows during low-traffic periods. Phase 5 Wave 5 and Closeout (2 months): mainframe-adjacent applications with special handling, data center decommission validation, Direct Connect replacement of VPN, FedRAMP continuous monitoring establishment.
target_output.development_guidelines: All Terraform modules in GitLab with semantic versioning; application team accounts pin to specific module versions not latest. tfsec and Checkov must pass with zero HIGH findings before merge to main. Terraform resource naming convention: {environment}-{service}-{region}-{sequence} enforced via Checkov custom rule. No hardcoded account IDs or ARNs in module code; all cross-account references via data sources or variable inputs. Ansible roles tested with Molecule against Amazon Linux 2023 and RHEL 9 target images. Python migration scripts require type annotations and pytest coverage above 80%. All new SCP policies reviewed by IT security officer and tested in Sandbox OU before applying to Workloads OU. Module changes require terraform plan output reviewed for unexpected resource replacements before apply approval.
target_output.risks_and_tradeoffs: Risk: FedRAMP ATO timeline slippage delays all production migrations. Mitigation: engage FedRAMP agency sponsor and 3PAO assessor in parallel with landing zone construction; begin SSP documentation in Phase 1 using AWS GovCloud inherited control narratives to minimize customer-responsible documentation effort. Risk: 3 mainframe-dependent applications may not complete within 18-month window due to mainframe integration complexity. Mitigation: assess mainframe dependency scope in Phase 1 discovery; negotiate data center co-location extension for mainframe-dependent systems if needed while migrating remaining 37+ workloads on schedule. Risk: Transit Gateway becomes a single point of network failure for all spoke VPCs. Mitigation: Transit Gateway is a managed service with AWS SLA; secondary attachment paths via redundant VPN connections; Network Firewall in separate AZs within inspection VPC. Tradeoff: shared EKS cluster over per-team clusters reduces cost by approximately 60% but introduces multi-tenancy isolation requirements; Kubernetes namespace isolation with network policies and OPA Gatekeeper admission control compensates.
target_output.open_questions_resolved: 4-OU Organizations structure selected over flat account model for compliance segmentation and SCP targeting flexibility. Shared EKS cluster in Infrastructure OU selected over per-application-team clusters to control cluster management costs at 40+ workload scale. Direct Connect selected for long-term hybrid connectivity over VPN; VPN used during migration period while Direct Connect circuit is provisioned. Compliance evidence collection automated via nightly Lambda rather than manual collection to meet FedRAMP continuous monitoring requirements. Application inventory tracked in YAML in GitLab rather than a separate CMDB system to maintain single source of truth with IaC.

metadata.notes: First-pass strong-quality state DOT cloud landing zone migration; FedRAMP Moderate ATO, 4-OU Organizations structure, 5-wave migration plan, Terraform module library architecture.

profile.domain: State Agency Compliance Monitoring - FISMA/NIST Cloud Configuration Scanning

project_goal.value: Continuously scan cloud resource configurations against CIS Benchmarks and NIST SP 800-53 controls, generate remediation tickets, and compile FISMA assessment evidence packages for state agencies
project_goal.rationale: Core mission: automate ConMon and reduce manual evidence collection burden

target_users.value: State agency FISMA officers, system owners, and security operations staff
target_users.rationale: Primary consumers of scan findings and evidence packages

project_class.rationale: Headless scanning and reporting tool with no interactive frontend

capabilities.rationale: Celery batch scanning, PostgreSQL findings storage, AWS/Azure API integrations, CI/CD-driven deployment

complexity_level.rationale: Multi-cloud API integrations, NIST control mapping, evidence package compilation, ITSM ticketing

risk_level.rationale: Tool holds cloud credentials and scan results containing security posture details

data_sensitivity.rationale: Scan findings reveal unpatched vulnerabilities and misconfiguration details that are sensitive government security data

external_exposure.rationale: Tool runs entirely within GovCloud; no external endpoints

access_model.value: IAM roles with least-privilege for cloud API access; operator access via Celery Flower and CLI restricted to VPN
access_model.rationale: Least-privilege prevents scan credentials from being used beyond read-only cloud APIs

feature_scope.value: Scheduled multi-cloud configuration scans, NIST control mapping, severity scoring, ITSM ticket creation, evidence package export, risk acceptance workflow, POA&M tracking
feature_scope.rationale: Full ConMon automation feature set

mvp_scope.value: AWS GovCloud scanning with CIS Benchmark checks, critical finding ITSM tickets, and basic evidence package export
mvp_scope.rationale: AWS-first because all pilot agencies are on GovCloud

security_baseline.value: IAM roles with read-only cloud permissions, all findings encrypted at rest in PostgreSQL with RDS encryption, TLS 1.3 for all API calls, audit log of all evidence exports
security_baseline.rationale: FISMA High baseline required for tool handling security posture data

frontend_stack.value: None - headless automation tool; Celery Flower for operator monitoring
frontend_stack.rationale: No interactive UI required; Flower provides task queue visibility

backend_stack.value: Python 3.12 with Celery 5.3
backend_stack.rationale: Celery provides distributed task scheduling with retry logic and beat scheduler for periodic scans

data_platform.value: PostgreSQL 16 on RDS GovCloud with encryption at rest
data_platform.rationale: Relational model for control mappings, findings, and remediation tracking

hosting_target.value: ECS Fargate on AWS GovCloud us-gov-west-1
hosting_target.rationale: FedRAMP-authorized compute with managed container runtime

privacy_retention_policy.value: Scan findings retained 3 years for FISMA audit trail; evidence packages retained 6 years per NARA schedule; risk acceptance records retained 7 years
privacy_retention_policy.rationale: Federal records retention requirements for security assessment artifacts

future_scope.value: Azure Government and GCP scanning, automated POA&M milestone tracking, AI-assisted remediation guidance
future_scope.rationale: Expand beyond AWS once pilot agencies validate core scanning accuracy

constraints.value: Scan API calls must use read-only IAM roles; no write access to scanned accounts; evidence packages must be exportable as structured JSON and Word DOCX
constraints.rationale: Read-only constraint prevents accidental resource modification in production agency accounts

observability_baseline.value: Datadog APM on Celery workers, structured JSON logs, PagerDuty alert on scan failure or evidence export error
observability_baseline.rationale: Corporate monitoring standard; scan failures must be detected before assessment deadlines

execution_preference.value: GitLab CI with feature branches; weekly sprint reviews with agency ISSO representatives
execution_preference.rationale: Agency stakeholder involvement needed to validate control mappings

compliance_context.value: FISMA, NIST SP 800-53 Rev 5, CIS Benchmarks v8, FedRAMP continuous monitoring requirements
compliance_context.rationale: Tool itself must be FISMA-compliant as it processes sensitive security configuration data

project.goal: Automate FISMA ConMon scanning and evidence package generation for state agencies
project.target_users: Agency FISMA officers and security operations staff

backend.framework: Celery 5.3 with Redis broker, boto3 for AWS APIs, azure-mgmt-monitor for Azure

security.auth: IAM roles with least-privilege read-only
security.transport: TLS 1.3 for all API calls and DB connections

data.cache: Redis 7 as Celery broker and result backend

constraints.cloud_access: Read-only IAM roles only; no write permissions to scanned accounts
constraints.export_formats: JSON and Word DOCX evidence packages

reasoner_reviews.product: The AWS-first MVP with CIS Benchmark scanning and basic ITSM ticket creation is the right scope for the pilot. Three agencies provide enough variation in cloud footprint to validate control mapping accuracy. Evidence package DOCX export is the highest-value feature for ISSO representatives who need assessment artifacts.
reasoner_reviews.architect_reasoner: Celery beat scheduler is appropriate for periodic scans. The round 2 plan improved NIST control mapping coverage but left the evidence package compilation logic underspecified. Round 3 must detail how the tool aggregates scan_findings across multiple assessment_periods into a coherent evidence package, particularly for partially-remediated controls that appear in multiple scan cycles.
reasoner_reviews.security: The tool processes confidential security posture data and holds read-only cloud credentials. Round 2 addressed IAM role isolation but left the evidence export audit trail incomplete -- every evidence package export must be logged with the requesting user, timestamp, and included finding IDs to support FISMA audit requirements. The round 2 plan did not address how exported packages are protected in transit.
reasoner_reviews.constraints: The DOCX export format requires a library dependency (python-docx) that adds a non-trivial attack surface. Round 3 should evaluate whether a structured JSON export satisfies all ISSO needs first, with DOCX as an optional rendering layer.

backend.service_design: Four Celery task modules: cloud_scanner (AWS Config + Security Hub polling), control_mapper (finding-to-NIST mapping engine), ticket_creator (ITSM ServiceNow integration), evidence_compiler (evidence package assembler)
backend.api_patterns: No public API; internal Celery task chaining; CLI entry points for manual scans and evidence export
backend.failure_handling: Celery retry with exponential backoff on AWS API throttling; dead-letter queue for failed tasks; PagerDuty alert on consecutive scan task failures

previous_plan.title: State Agency FISMA Compliance Scanner v2
previous_plan.executive_summary: Celery-based automation tool scanning AWS GovCloud resources against CIS Benchmarks and NIST SP 800-53 controls, generating ITSM tickets for critical findings and compiling evidence packages for annual FISMA assessments.
previous_plan.architecture_overview: Four Celery task modules on ECS Fargate: cloud_scanner polls AWS Config and Security Hub, control_mapper maps findings to NIST controls, ticket_creator integrates with ServiceNow, evidence_compiler assembles assessment artifacts.
previous_plan.data_model: compliance_controls, resource_configurations, scan_findings, remediation_tickets, evidence_artifacts, assessment_periods, risk_acceptance_records, poa_items stored in RDS PostgreSQL GovCloud.
previous_plan.security_and_compliance: ECS task role with read-only IAM; audit_exports table logs every export with user ARN and S3 key; TLS 1.3 for all API calls.

target_output.thinking_summary: Round 3 addresses ISS-001 by specifying the evidence_compiler aggregation algorithm: for each NIST control in scope, select the latest scan_finding per resource, attach the full remediation history from all prior assessment_periods where that control appeared, and include any active risk_acceptance_record or poa_item. This gives assessors a complete per-control evidence narrative. ISS-003 is addressed by adopting JSON as the primary export format with DOCX as an optional rendering step using python-docx with template-based generation (no user-supplied templates accepted) and input sanitization before injection into the template.

fix_report.action_taken: Defined evidence_compiler aggregation algorithm: for each control in assessment scope, query latest scan_finding per resource_arn, join remediation_tickets and risk_acceptance_records for that control across all prior assessment_periods, compute control_status (satisfied/partially_satisfied/not_satisfied) based on finding counts, and serialize to evidence_artifact JSONB. Added compile_evidence_package Celery task signature with explicit inputs and outputs.
fix_report.expected_outcome: Evidence packages contain complete per-control remediation history enabling assessors to evaluate NIST control satisfaction without manual data collection

fix_report.action_taken: Adopted JSON as primary export format serialized from evidence_artifact records; DOCX rendering is an optional post-processing step using python-docx with a fixed server-side template (no user-supplied templates); all string fields sanitized with bleach before template injection; python-docx pinned to audited version in requirements.txt with Dependabot alerts enabled.
fix_report.expected_outcome: JSON export satisfies primary ISSO consumption needs; DOCX rendering uses fixed template eliminating template injection risk; Dependabot monitors python-docx for CVEs

target_output.title: State Agency FISMA Compliance Scanner v3
target_output.executive_summary: Python Celery automation tool on ECS Fargate GovCloud scanning AWS Config and Security Hub findings against CIS Benchmarks and NIST SP 800-53 Rev 5 controls for state agency FISMA continuous monitoring. Round 3 resolves evidence package compilation logic and DOCX export security, producing complete per-control remediation history packages for annual FISMA assessments.
target_output.architecture_overview: Four Celery task modules deployed as ECS Fargate services on AWS GovCloud: cloud_scanner polls AWS Config rule evaluation results and Security Hub findings via boto3 every 4 hours per agency; control_mapper runs after each scan to join findings to the NIST SP 800-53 control library and compute severity-weighted compliance scores; ticket_creator posts critical and high findings to ServiceNow via REST; evidence_compiler assembles structured per-control evidence packages aggregating findings across all prior assessment_periods. PostgreSQL 16 on RDS stores all findings, mappings, and audit records. Redis on ElastiCache serves as Celery broker and result backend. S3 GovCloud stores compiled evidence package artifacts. Datadog APM instruments all Celery tasks.
target_output.technology_stack: Python 3.12, Celery 5.3 with Redis broker, boto3 for AWS Config and Security Hub APIs, azure-mgmt-monitor for Azure Government (phase 2), psycopg3 for async PostgreSQL, python-docx 1.1 (pinned) for optional DOCX rendering, bleach for input sanitization before template injection. PostgreSQL 16 on RDS GovCloud with AES-256 encryption at rest. Redis 7 on ElastiCache. S3 GovCloud for evidence artifacts. ECS Fargate GovCloud, ECR GovCloud, GitLab CI, Datadog, PagerDuty.
target_output.functional_feature_map: Cloud Scanner: scheduled boto3 calls to AWS Config GetComplianceDetailsByConfigRule and SecurityHub GetFindings every 4 hours per agency account, writing resource_configuration and scan_finding rows. Control Mapper: post-scan task joining findings to compliance_controls table using CIS-to-NIST mapping JSON, computing control_status per resource. Ticket Creator: Celery task watching for new critical/high scan_findings and POSTing ServiceNow incidents with finding detail, affected resource ARN, and remediation guidance. Evidence Compiler: on-demand task aggregating latest findings per resource per control with full remediation history, serializing to evidence_artifact JSONB, generating presigned S3 URL. Risk Acceptance Workflow: ISSO approves risk_acceptance_record for accepted exceptions, linked to scan_findings by control_id. POA&M Tracker: poa_items linked to unresolved high/critical findings with milestone dates and status.
target_output.system_components: cloud_scanner ECS service (3 Fargate tasks for parallel agency scans; ECS task role with read-only IAM: config:GetComplianceDetailsByConfigRule, securityhub:GetFindings, ec2:DescribeInstances, s3:GetBucketPolicy). control_mapper ECS service (1 Fargate task triggered by Celery chain after each scan completion). ticket_creator ECS service (1 task polling for unticketted critical/high findings). evidence_compiler ECS service (on-demand Fargate task triggered by CLI or scheduled pre-assessment run). beat_scheduler ECS service (1 task running Celery beat for 4-hour scan cron). RDS PostgreSQL 16 in private subnet with RDS encryption. ElastiCache Redis 7 (Celery broker + result backend). S3 GovCloud bucket for evidence_artifacts with server-side encryption and versioning. Datadog agent sidecar on all Fargate tasks.
target_output.workflows: Scan-to-Ticket Pipeline: Celery beat fires scan_agency task every 4 hours; cloud_scanner polls AWS Config and Security Hub for each agency account via cross-account IAM assume_role, writes resource_configurations and scan_findings; Celery chain triggers map_controls task which joins findings to compliance_controls and updates control_status; for each new critical or high finding with no open ticket, ticket_creator POSTs ServiceNow incident with resource ARN, control_id, CIS benchmark reference, and remediation steps; finding record updated with ticket_id. Evidence Package Compilation: ISSO triggers compile_evidence_package CLI command specifying agency_id and assessment_period_id; evidence_compiler queries latest scan_finding per resource_arn per control_id in scope; for each control joins remediation_tickets, risk_acceptance_records, and poa_items from all prior assessment_periods; computes control_status ENUM satisfied/partially_satisfied/not_satisfied; serializes to evidence_artifact JSONB and writes to S3; logs export to audit_exports with ISSO user ARN, timestamp, assessment_period_id, S3 key, and finding_ids list; returns presigned URL valid 24 hours.
target_output.data_model: compliance_controls (id UUID PK, control_id VARCHAR UNIQUE e.g. AC-2, family VARCHAR, title VARCHAR, nist_rev VARCHAR, cis_benchmark_refs JSONB). resource_configurations (id UUID PK, agency_id UUID FK, resource_arn VARCHAR, resource_type VARCHAR, configuration_json JSONB, scanned_at TIMESTAMPTZ). scan_findings (id UUID PK, resource_config_id UUID FK, control_id UUID FK, severity ENUM critical/high/medium/low, status ENUM open/resolved/accepted, finding_detail TEXT, first_seen_at TIMESTAMPTZ, last_seen_at TIMESTAMPTZ, ticket_id VARCHAR). remediation_tickets (id UUID PK, finding_id UUID FK, itsm_ticket_id VARCHAR, created_at TIMESTAMPTZ, resolved_at TIMESTAMPTZ, resolution_notes TEXT). evidence_artifacts (id UUID PK, assessment_period_id UUID FK, agency_id UUID FK, compiled_at TIMESTAMPTZ, artifact_json JSONB, s3_key VARCHAR, docx_s3_key VARCHAR). assessment_periods (id UUID PK, agency_id UUID FK, period_start DATE, period_end DATE, status ENUM open/closed). risk_acceptance_records (id UUID PK, control_id UUID FK, agency_id UUID FK, accepted_by VARCHAR, rationale TEXT, accepted_at TIMESTAMPTZ, expires_at TIMESTAMPTZ). poa_items (id UUID PK, finding_id UUID FK, milestone_date DATE, status ENUM open/in_progress/closed, responsible_party VARCHAR). audit_exports (id UUID PK, exported_by_arn VARCHAR, assessment_period_id UUID FK, s3_key VARCHAR, finding_ids UUID[], exported_at TIMESTAMPTZ).
target_output.api_design: No public HTTP API. CLI entry points via Python Click: scan-agency --agency-id UUID --account-id (triggers on-demand scan bypassing schedule). compile-evidence --agency-id UUID --period-id UUID --format json|docx (compiles and returns presigned S3 URL). list-findings --agency-id UUID --severity critical|high|medium|low --status open (prints findings table). accept-risk --finding-id UUID --rationale TEXT --expires DATE (creates risk_acceptance_record). update-poam --poam-id UUID --status open|in_progress|closed. All CLI commands require AWS SSO session token; audit_exports logged for compile-evidence. Celery Flower at flower.internal:5555 on VPN, IP-restricted via ALB security group.
target_output.security_and_compliance: ECS task role least-privilege: config:GetComplianceDetailsByConfigRule, securityhub:GetFindings, ec2:Describe*, s3:GetBucketPolicy -- no write permissions to scanned accounts. Cross-account access via assume_role to read-only scanning role in each agency account. RDS encrypted with AWS KMS CMK; TLS 1.3 for all DB connections. S3 evidence bucket SSE-KMS with bucket policy denying non-TLS access. Every compile-evidence execution logged to audit_exports with user ARN, timestamp, period_id, s3_key, and finding_ids list. DOCX rendering: fixed server-side python-docx template (no user-supplied templates); all string fields sanitized with bleach.clean() before injection; python-docx 1.1 pinned in requirements.txt with Dependabot alerts. FISMA compliance: tool itself operates under an ATO with annual review; control evidence covers AC-2, AU-2, AU-12, CA-7, RA-5, SI-2.
target_output.deployment_and_operations: ECS Fargate in us-gov-west-1 private subnets. Services: cloud_scanner (3 tasks, 2 vCPU / 4GB each), control_mapper (1 task on-demand via Celery), ticket_creator (1 task, 0.5 vCPU), beat_scheduler (1 task, 0.25 vCPU), evidence_compiler (on-demand Fargate task spawned per request). ECR GovCloud for container images. RDS PostgreSQL 16 Multi-AZ in db.r7g.large. ElastiCache Redis 7 cluster.t4g.medium. GitLab CI: mypy + pytest --cov=80% on PR; Docker build and ECR push on merge to main; ECS rolling update with health check gate; integration tests via LocalStack for AWS API mocking. Rollback: ECS previous task definition revision. Secrets Manager for ServiceNow API key and database password with 30-day rotation.
target_output.observability: Datadog APM tracing on all Celery task functions with task_name, agency_id, and control_family tags. Custom metrics: scan_findings_by_severity counters per agency, scan_duration_seconds histogram, ticket_creation_success_rate gauge, evidence_compile_duration_seconds histogram. PagerDuty: CRITICAL alert if scan_agency task fails 3 consecutive runs for any agency (assessment deadline risk); WARNING if ticket_creation_success_rate < 0.95 over 1 hour. CloudWatch Logs for ECS task stdout; Datadog log forwarder enriches with task ARN and agency_id. S3 access logs for evidence bucket. CloudTrail enabled on all GovCloud accounts including scanning role assume_role events.
target_output.cost_and_scaling: ~$400/month for 3 pilot agencies: ECS Fargate tasks $180, RDS db.r7g.large $160, ElastiCache $30, S3 + data transfer $30. Scales linearly with agency count; 20 agencies estimated at $1,200/month. Scan parallelism scales by adding cloud_scanner task replicas. RDS read replica added if reporting queries impact scan write throughput.
target_output.phased_implementation: Phase 1 - AWS Pilot (Q2, 3 agencies): Deploy cloud_scanner for AWS Config and Security Hub, control_mapper for CIS-to-NIST mapping, ticket_creator for ServiceNow, evidence_compiler with JSON export; validate mapping accuracy with agency ISSOs; baseline FISMA assessment evidence packages. Phase 2 - Azure Government (Q3): Add azure-mgmt-monitor scanner module; extend control_mapper for Azure-specific findings; validate with 2 Azure-using agencies. Phase 3 - Advanced Features (Q4): AI-assisted remediation guidance in ticket descriptions, automated POA&M milestone reminders, multi-agency dashboard in Celery Flower or lightweight Flask admin.
target_output.development_guidelines: Python: mypy strict mode enforced in CI; all boto3 calls wrapped in retry decorator with exponential backoff for ThrottlingException. Celery: all tasks idempotent (scan re-runs produce same scan_finding rows via upsert on resource_arn + control_id + scan_date); no task stores sensitive data in result backend beyond 1 hour TTL. DOCX rendering: only use fixed template strings; never interpolate user input directly; all fields pass bleach.clean() before insertion. Secrets: never log AWS credentials, ServiceNow tokens, or database passwords; use Secrets Manager SDK calls only.
target_output.risks_and_tradeoffs: Risk: read-only IAM role credentials in ECS task environment could be exfiltrated via container escape. Mitigation: ECS task metadata endpoint IMDSv2 required; no credentials stored in environment variables; credentials via instance metadata only. Risk: NIST control mapping errors cause false-negative evidence packages. Mitigation: mapping JSON reviewed by agency ISSOs during Phase 1 pilot; version-controlled with PR review gate. Tradeoff: Celery over AWS Step Functions -- Celery provides richer retry and chain semantics; Step Functions would simplify IAM but add SDK complexity for multi-cloud expansion. Tradeoff: JSON-primary over DOCX-primary -- JSON is machine-readable for future API consumers; DOCX rendering deferred to optional post-processing.
target_output.open_questions_resolved: Confirmed JSON-primary export satisfies ISSO review needs based on pilot agency feedback; DOCX offered as optional convenience. Chose ServiceNow over Jira for ITSM tickets because all pilot agencies use ServiceNow. Confirmed 4-hour scan interval meets FedRAMP continuous monitoring frequency requirement for moderate baseline.

metadata.notes: Round 3 resolving evidence compilation logic and DOCX export security for FISMA compliance scanner.

profile.domain: County Government - Zero-Trust Network Segmentation and Privileged Access Management

project_goal.value: Implement zero-trust network segmentation, privileged access management, and endpoint detection and response across all county government departments
project_goal.rationale: Reduce lateral movement risk after county ransomware incident; satisfy cyber insurance requirements

target_users.value: County IT administrators, department system owners, and security operations center analysts
target_users.rationale: Primary operators and consumers of the hardened infrastructure

project_class.rationale: Platform and environment architecture project, not an application

capabilities.rationale: Terraform IaC, Ansible configuration management, PAM and EDR integrations

complexity_level.rationale: Multi-department rollout, hybrid cloud and on-premise scope, PAM and microsegmentation coordination

risk_level.rationale: Misconfigured firewall rules or PAM policies could disrupt critical county services including 911 dispatch and court systems

data_sensitivity.rationale: Network topology, privileged account inventory, and vulnerability findings are sensitive government security data

external_exposure.rationale: All infrastructure management interfaces restricted to county intranet and VPN

access_model.value: Azure AD Conditional Access with device compliance check; PAM dual-approval for all privileged account requests; MFA required for all Azure AD users
access_model.rationale: Zero-trust requires verified identity and device health before granting access

feature_scope.value: Network microsegmentation via Azure Firewall, PAM with session recording and dual-approval, EDR deployment on all endpoints, vulnerability management integration, identity federation for cross-department SSO
feature_scope.rationale: Full zero-trust maturity model scope for cyber insurance compliance

mvp_scope.value: Microsegmentation for top-3 critical network zones (911/dispatch, finance, IT admin), PAM deployment for all domain admin accounts, EDR on 80% of endpoints
mvp_scope.rationale: Prioritize highest-risk zones for cyber insurance deadline

security_baseline.value: CIS Controls v8, CISA Zero Trust Maturity Model Level 2, all privileged sessions recorded, vulnerability scans weekly
security_baseline.rationale: Baseline required for county cyber insurance renewal

frontend_stack.value: None - infrastructure project; Microsoft Sentinel SIEM console and PAM web portal for operator access
frontend_stack.rationale: No custom frontend; commercial tool consoles used

backend_stack.value: Terraform 1.8 with AzureRM provider and Ansible 2.17
backend_stack.rationale: Terraform for Azure resource provisioning; Ansible for endpoint and on-premise configuration

data_platform.value: PostgreSQL 16 for PAM audit database and infrastructure inventory
data_platform.rationale: Relational store for privileged session audit records and network segment inventory

hosting_target.value: Azure Government + on-premise Hyper-V; hybrid connectivity via ExpressRoute
hosting_target.rationale: County has existing Azure Government tenant and on-premise data centers connected via ExpressRoute

privacy_retention_policy.value: PAM session recordings retained 1 year; audit logs retained 7 years per county records schedule; vulnerability scan results retained 3 years
privacy_retention_policy.rationale: County records retention ordinance and cyber insurance policy requirements

future_scope.value: Zero-trust maturity level 3 (continuous validation), software-defined WAN for branch offices, automated patch remediation
future_scope.rationale: Year 2 roadmap after insurance compliance achieved

constraints.value: Must not disrupt 911 dispatch or court case management systems during rollout; change windows restricted to 2am-5am weekday maintenance windows; on-premise Hyper-V hosts cannot be rebooted during court session hours
constraints.rationale: Critical county services require uninterrupted operation

observability_baseline.value: Microsoft Sentinel SIEM for security event correlation; Azure Monitor for infrastructure metrics; PagerDuty for critical alerts
observability_baseline.rationale: Existing Sentinel investment; SOC analysts already trained on Sentinel workbooks

execution_preference.value: Azure DevOps Pipelines for IaC promotion; weekly change advisory board review for production changes
execution_preference.rationale: County change management process requires CAB approval for production network changes

compliance_context.value: CISA Zero Trust Maturity Model, CIS Controls v8, state IT security standards, county cyber insurance policy requirements
compliance_context.rationale: Cyber insurance renewal contingent on demonstrating zero-trust controls by Q3

project.goal: Implement zero-trust segmentation, PAM, and EDR across county infrastructure
project.target_users: County IT admins, SOC analysts, department system owners
project.timeline: Q3 deadline for cyber insurance renewal

security.auth: Azure AD Conditional Access with device compliance; PAM dual-approval
security.transport: ExpressRoute for Azure-to-on-premise; TLS 1.3 for management interfaces

data.db: PostgreSQL 16 for PAM audit and infrastructure inventory

constraints.change_windows: 2am-5am weekday only; no Hyper-V reboots during court hours
constraints.critical_systems: 911 dispatch and court case management must not be disrupted

reasoner_reviews.product: The MVP scope targeting 911/dispatch, finance, and IT admin zones first is correct prioritization for the cyber insurance deadline. The round 1 plan established the correct three-zone segmentation structure but left the PAM vendor decision unresolved, which blocks the PAM dual-approval pipeline design.
reasoner_reviews.architect_reasoner: Terraform AzureRM for Azure Firewall policy rules is the right pattern, with Ansible for on-premise Hyper-V SDN configuration and EDR agent deployment. The round 1 plan did not detail the Azure Firewall rule hierarchy for inter-zone traffic -- round 2 must specify which cross-zone flows are explicitly permitted (e.g., finance-to-AD auth, dispatch-to-logging) versus denied by default.
reasoner_reviews.security: The round 1 plan acknowledged PAM dual-approval but did not specify session recording storage and access controls. PAM session recordings contain privileged operator activity and must be stored in an access-restricted Azure Blob container with separate RBAC from the PAM administrators themselves to prevent cover-up of malicious admin activity.
reasoner_reviews.constraints: The 2am-5am change window constraint requires Terraform plan/apply pipelines to have a scheduled-run gate. The round 1 plan used a manual apply step; round 2 should implement a pipeline schedule gate preventing applies outside the approved window except for emergency break-glass procedures.

backend.service_design: Terraform modules: network_segmentation (Azure Firewall policies, NSGs, route tables), pam_infrastructure (CyberArk vault VMs, session gateway), edr_deployment (Defender for Endpoint onboarding). Ansible playbooks: hyper_v_sdn (on-premise host configuration), edr_agent (cross-platform EDR installation)
backend.api_patterns: No application API; Terraform state in Azure Blob backend with state locking; Ansible dynamic inventory from Azure Resource Manager
backend.failure_handling: Terraform plan validation in CI before apply; Ansible --check mode dry-run before live execution; rollback runbooks for each Terraform module

devops.deployment_topology: Azure Government: Hub VNet with Azure Firewall, three spoke VNets (911-dispatch, finance, it-admin) connected via VNet peering, ExpressRoute gateway for on-premise connectivity. On-premise: Hyper-V cluster with SDN for VM network segmentation.
devops.ci_cd_design: Azure DevOps Pipelines: terraform validate + tflint on PR; terraform plan output as PR comment; scheduled apply gate (2am-5am window enforced via pipeline condition); Ansible lint + --check dry-run before playbook execution
devops.observability_stack: Microsoft Sentinel workspace receiving Azure Firewall logs, Azure AD sign-in logs, Defender for Endpoint alerts, and PAM session events; KQL workbooks for zero-trust posture score and privileged access anomalies; PagerDuty integration for P1 Sentinel incidents

security.auth_design: Azure AD Conditional Access Policy requiring MFA + Intune-compliant device for all access; break-glass accounts in dedicated emergency access group with no Conditional Access exclusions except the break-glass policy itself
security.authorization_model: Custom Azure RBAC roles: NetworkSegmentationContributor (write Azure Firewall rules), PAMAdministrator (manage CyberArk vault), SOCAnalyst (read Sentinel, read PAM audit), EndpointManager (Defender for Endpoint)
security.audit_and_logging_controls: Azure Monitor diagnostic settings forwarding Azure Firewall, NSG flow logs, and Azure AD to Sentinel Log Analytics workspace; PAM session recordings in restricted Azure Blob with separate read RBAC

ISS-001.title: Azure Firewall inter-zone permit rules not specified
ISS-001.detail: Round 1 plan established three network zones but did not enumerate which cross-zone flows are explicitly permitted. Default-deny is correct but without documented permit rules the segmentation policy is incomplete and could block legitimate critical flows like finance-to-AD authentication.

history.note: Raised by architect_reasoner in round 1 audit

ISS-002.title: Terraform apply pipeline lacks change window enforcement
ISS-002.detail: Round 1 plan uses manual apply step with no automated gate preventing applies outside the 2am-5am maintenance window. An engineer could apply production network changes during business hours, risking disruption to 911 dispatch.

history.note: Raised by constraints reviewer in round 1 audit

focus_issues.title: Azure Firewall inter-zone permit rules not specified
focus_issues.detail: Cross-zone permit rules must be enumerated to avoid blocking legitimate critical flows.

focus_issues.title: Terraform apply pipeline lacks change window enforcement
focus_issues.detail: No automated gate preventing production applies outside 2am-5am maintenance window.

latest_recommendations: Enumerate Azure Firewall Application and Network rules for legitimate inter-zone flows before deploying default-deny policy
Add pipeline schedule condition in Azure DevOps to block terraform apply stage outside 2am-5am window; create separate break-glass pipeline with CAB approval gate for emergency applies
